congeal: Blood began to congeal at wound site.

insuperable: The committee found the logistical challenges insuperable within the given timeline.
internecine: Destructive to both sides in a conflict; within a group
Internecine conflicts within the ruling coalition weakened the government.
interpolate: To insert words into a text; to introduce new data into a sequence
The court found that passages had been interpolated into the original document to falsify its meaning.
intractable: Hard to control or deal with; difficult to solve
Poverty in remote tribal areas remains an intractable challenge for policymakers.
intransigent: Unwilling or refusing to change one's views or to agree
The intransigent stance of both parties prevented negotiations.
intrepid: The intrepid journalist exposed corruption at great personal risk.
invective: Insulting, abusive, or highly critical language
The parliamentary session dissolved into mutual invective with no legislation passed.
inveterate: Having a particular habit or activity so firmly established it is unlikely to change
The inveterate corruption in procurement departments persisted despite multiple audits.
irascible: Having or showing a tendency to be easily angered
The irascible magistrate was known for his sharp rebukes in open court.
irrefutable: Impossible to deny or disprove; undeniable
The DNA evidence provided irrefutable proof of the accused's presence at the scene.
irresolute: An irresolute leader emboldens adversaries and demoralises supporters.
itinerant: Travelling from place to place; nomadic
Itinerant vendors were relocated to organised markets under the Smart Cities Mission.
jargon: Special words or expressions used by a particular group that are difficult for others to understand
Legal jargon must be simplified so that ordinary citizens can understand their rights.
jejune: Naive, simplistic, and superficial; lacking nourishment
The report's jejune analysis failed to account for structural economic factors.
jingoism: Extreme patriotism in the form of aggressive foreign policy; chauvinism
Responsible statesmanship must be distinguished from jingoism that inflames public sentiment.
jocular: Fond of joking; humorous and playful
The judge's jocular aside lightened the tense atmosphere without diminishing the gravity of the case.
juggernaut: A large, heavy, and powerful force that crushes everything in its path
The economic juggernaut of China reshaped global supply chains in two decades.
juncture: A particular point in events or time; a place where things join
At this critical juncture, India must choose between short-term populism and long-term reform.
jurisdiction: The official power to make legal decisions; the territory over which authority extends
The Supreme Court has jurisdiction over all constitutional matters in India.
juxtapose: To place close together for contrasting effect
The documentary juxtaposes urban and rural lifestyles in India.
juxtaposition: The fact of placing two things close together for contrasting effect
The juxtaposition of extreme wealth and abject poverty in Mumbai is stark.
knave: A dishonest or unscrupulous man; a rogue
The court unmasked the knave who had forged land records to defraud villagers.
knell: The sound of a bell rung solemnly for a death or funeral; a warning of the end
The Supreme Court's verdict sounded the death knell for the controversial ordinance.
kowtow: To act in an excessively subservient manner; to kneel and touch the ground
A strong judiciary must never kowtow to executive pressure.
kudos: Praise and honour received for an achievement
ISRO received global kudos for the successful Chandrayaan-3 lunar landing.
labyrinthine: Complicated and difficult to navigate; like a labyrinth
The labyrinthine tax code discouraged honest compliance among small businesses.
lachrymose: Tending to cry frequently; tearful; inducing tears
The lachrymose testimony of the survivor moved even the stern judges.
lackadaisical: Lacking enthusiasm and determination; carelessly lazy
The lackadaisical enforcement of traffic rules contributed to rising accident rates.
laconic: Using very few words; brief and to the point
The army commander gave laconic orders during the operation.
lampoon: To publicly criticise with satire; a satirical attack
Political cartoonists lampoon leaders to hold power accountable through humour.
languid: Displaying or having a disinclination for physical exertion; weak
The languid pace of judicial reforms frustrated victims seeking timely justice.
lassitude: Physical or mental weariness; lack of energy
The lassitude of bureaucratic processes drained the enthusiasm of reform-minded officers.
laudatory: The committee's laudatory report on the scheme glossed over its implementation failures.
lethargic: Affected by lethargy; sluggish and apathetic
The lethargic response of the administration to the drought warnings cost thousands of lives.
levity: The treatment of a serious matter with humour; lightness
The judge admonished the lawyer for bringing levity into such a grave matter.
libertarian: Advocating maximum individual freedom with minimal state intervention
The libertarian critique of welfare schemes focuses on dependency rather than empowerment.
lien: A legal right to keep possession of property until a debt is paid
The bank placed a lien on the property until the defaulted loan was repaid.
lionise: To treat someone as a celebrity or person of great importance
The media lionised the young IAS officer who transformed her district's sanitation record.
litigious: Prone to engaging in lawsuits; excessively fond of legal disputes
The litigious nature of Indian land disputes clogs courts with cases spanning generations.
loquacious: Tending to talk a great deal; talkative
The loquacious witness gave lengthy testimony in court.
loquacity: The quality of talking a great deal; talkativeness
The witness's loquacity produced hours of testimony but little useful evidence.
lugubrious: Looking or sounding sad and dismal; mournful
The lugubrious tone of the minister's speech confirmed the gravity of the economic crisis.
macabre: Disturbing and horrifying because of its involvement with death
The macabre details of the mass grave shocked international human rights observers.
machination: A plot or scheme, especially one that is intricate and underhand
The machinations of the rival faction brought down the coalition government.
magnanimous: Generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival or less powerful person
The leader showed magnanimous gesture by forgiving his opponents.
magniloquent: The magniloquent manifesto promised everything but delivered nothing.
maladroit: The maladroit handling of the diplomatic crisis embarrassed the foreign ministry.
malapropism: The mistaken use of a word in place of a similar-sounding one
The politician's malapropism caused laughter in the assembly when he said 'illiterate' instead of 'literate'.
malediction: A curse; calling down evil upon someone
The wrongfully convicted man's malediction against corrupt officials echoed in the courtroom.
malfeasance: Wrongdoing, especially by a public official
The collector was suspended pending inquiry into allegations of malfeasance.
manifesto: A public declaration of policy, aims, and intentions by a political party
The party's manifesto promised land reform but the government quietly shelved it after winning.
marginalise: To treat a person or group as insignificant or peripheral
Constitutional safeguards exist precisely to prevent the majority from marginalising minorities.
martinet: A strict disciplinarian, especially in the armed forces
The new commandant was a martinet who insisted on letter-perfect adherence to regulations.
maverick: An independent-minded person who does not conform
The maverick scientist challenged established theories in physics.
mellifluous: Sweet or musical; pleasant to hear
The singer's mellifluous voice captivated the audience.
mendacious: The commission found the minister's testimony mendacious and contradicted by documents.
mendicant: A beggar; a member of a mendicant order who depends on charity
The state's failure to rehabilitate mendicants reflects gaps in social welfare delivery.
mercurial: Subject to sudden or unpredictable changes of mood; volatile
The mercurial temperament of the chief minister made policy continuity difficult.
meretricious: Apparently attractive but having no real value; showy
The meretricious promises in the election manifesto evaporated after the party won.
meritocracy: Government by those selected according to merit; a system valuing ability
India's UPSC system is intended to be a meritocracy, selecting officers on the basis of ability.
metamorphose: To change or cause to change completely in form or nature
India's cities have metamorphosed dramatically over the last three decades of economic growth.
metamorphosis: A change of form or nature; a complete transformation
The metamorphosis of India's digital economy over the last decade has been remarkable.
minatory: Expressing or conveying a threat; menacing
The minatory tone of the diplomatic note raised fears of military escalation.
misanthrope: A person who dislikes humankind and avoids human society
Years of corruption and betrayal turned the idealistic reformer into a misanthrope.
misnomer: A wrong or inaccurate name or designation
Calling India a 'developing' country is now almost a misnomer given its economic scale.
misprision: Deliberate concealment of one's knowledge of a crime; contempt or neglect of duty
Officers accused of misprision of treason face severe penalties under the law.
missive: A letter, especially a long or official one
The governor sent a formal missive to the Chief Minister demanding a floor test.
mitigate: To make less severe, serious, or painful
The court considered mitigating circumstances before passing a lenient sentence.
modicum: A small quantity of a particular thing
A modicum of transparency would restore public confidence in the administration.
mollify: To appease the anger or anxiety of someone
The government's relief package failed to mollify the aggrieved farming community.
monolithic: Forming a single, large, imposing structure; uniform and inflexible
The monolithic bureaucratic structure resisted every attempt at decentralisation.
moot: Subject to debate, dispute, or uncertainty; having no practical significance
Whether the ordinance was legal became a moot point after it was repealed.
moribund: In a dying state; near death; stagnant
The moribund public sector units were revived through strategic disinvestment.
morose: The morose silence of the prime minister after the election loss said more than words.
mortify: To cause someone to feel embarrassed or ashamed; to discipline by self-denial
The officer was mortified when the audit report was read aloud in Parliament.
munificent: Larger or more generous than is usual or necessary
The munificent endowment established the country's first fully funded rural university.
mutable: Public opinion is mutable and can shift dramatically with a single viral event.
myopic: Short-sighted; lacking imagination or intellectual insight
The myopic focus on short-term growth ignored long-term environmental costs.
myriad: A countless or extremely large number; innumerable
India's myriad languages, cultures, and traditions are its greatest strength.
narcissism: Excessive interest in oneself and one's physical appearance; vanity
The narcissism of leaders who prioritise personal image over public welfare is dangerous.
nascent: Just coming into existence and beginning to develop
India's nascent space economy is attracting global investment and talent.
nebulous: Not clear or defined; vague and hazy
The policy framework remained nebulous, offering no concrete targets or timelines.
nefarious: The police uncovered a nefarious plot to defraud investors.
nemesis: A long-standing rival or opponent; retributive justice
Corruption proved to be the nemesis of the once-popular chief minister.
neologism: A newly coined word or phrase; the coining of new words
Words like 'demonetisation' and 'digitise' are neologisms that entered common usage recently.
nepotism: The practice of favouring relatives or friends in professional appointments
Nepotism in civil service appointments undermines the principle of merit-based selection.
nexus: A connection or series of connections linking two or more things
The nexus between politicians and builders corrupted the urban planning process.
nihilism: The rejection of all religious and moral principles; belief in nothing
The spread of nihilism among disillusioned youth reflects a deeper crisis of governance.
nihilistic: Rejecting all moral and religious principles; believing nothing has meaning
The nihilistic rhetoric of extremist groups appeals to alienated youth.
nocturnal: Done or active at night; relating to the night
Nocturnal raids by forest officials disrupted illegal timber smuggling networks.
nocuous: Nocuous effluents from chemical plants destroyed aquatic life in the river.
nomenclature: The devising or choosing of names for things; a set of names or terms
The nomenclature of Indian administrative units reflects both colonial and indigenous traditions.
nonchalance: A state of being calm and relaxed, often to the point of indifference
His nonchalance during the parliamentary grilling unnerved the opposition.
nonchalant: Feeling or appearing casually calm and relaxed; not displaying anxiety
She maintained a nonchalant attitude despite the crisis.
noncompliance: Failure to act in accordance with a wish or command
States in noncompliance with Supreme Court orders were issued contempt notices.
normative: Relating to an evaluative standard; prescribing norms
Constitutional jurisprudence is both descriptive and normative in character.
notoriety: The state of being famous or well-known for a bad quality or deed
The scamster gained notoriety after his Ponzi scheme defrauded thousands of depositors.
noxious: Industrial units discharging noxious effluents into rivers were sealed by the tribunal.
obdurate: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action
The obdurate official refused to reconsider the decision despite evidence.
obfuscate: To make something unclear or difficult to understand
The complex legal language obfuscated the real issues.
oblivion: The state of being unaware or forgotten; complete forgetfulness
Once dominant regional parties have faded into political oblivion.
obsequious: Obedient or attentive to an excessive degree
The obsequious behavior of the assistant annoyed everyone.
obsequiousness: Excessive eagerness to please or serve; servility
The obsequiousness of advisors around the leader prevented honest feedback reaching him.
officious: Asserting authority in an overbearing way; too eager to serve or advise
The officious clerk refused to accept the application citing a trivial technicality.
oligarchy: A small group of people having control of a country or organisation
Critics argue that economic policy is shaped by an oligarchy of corporate interests.
omnipotent: Having unlimited power; able to do anything
No institution in a democracy should be omnipotent — all must be subject to checks.
omniscient: No government is omniscient — evidence-based policymaking must fill the gaps.
onus: Used to refer to something that is one's duty or responsibility
The onus of proving innocence must never be placed on the accused in criminal law.
opportunism: The practice of exploiting circumstances with little regard for principles
Political opportunism rather than ideology drove the unexpected coalition formation.
opprobrium: Harsh criticism or censure; public disgrace
The state government faced international opprobrium for its handling of the minority protests.
opulent: Ostentatiously rich and luxurious or lavish
The opulent palace reflected the maharaja's vast wealth.
oscillate: To move or swing back and forth; to waver between extremes
Public opinion continued to oscillate between support and opposition for the economic reforms.
ostensible: Stated or appearing to be true, but not necessarily so
The ostensible reason for the meeting was different from the actual agenda.
overwrought: In a state of nervous excitement or anxiety; too elaborate
The overwrought response of the government to media criticism revealed deeper insecurity.
painstaking: Done with or employing great care and thoroughness
The forensic team conducted a painstaking examination of the crime scene.
palatial: Resembling a palace in being spacious and splendid
The palatial government bungalows occupied by officers stand in contrast to the housing crisis.
palliate: To make a disease or its symptoms less severe without removing the cause
Medicines can palliate symptoms but not cure the underlying condition.
palliative: Relieving pain without dealing with the cause; a palliative measure
The food subsidy was a palliative measure that addressed hunger without solving poverty.
pallid: Pale, especially because of poor health; lacking vitality
The pallid complexion of children in anaemia-affected districts alarmed health workers.
paltry: Small or meagre; not worth considering
The paltry compensation offered to flood victims was widely criticised.
panacea: A solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases
Education is often viewed as a panacea for social problems.
panegyric: A public speech or text in praise of someone
The minister's retirement function became an occasion for panegyrics rather than honest assessment.
paradigm: A typical example or pattern of something; a model
The discovery created a new paradigm in scientific research.
paradigmatic: Of or relating to a paradigm; serving as a typical example
The Narmada Dam controversy was paradigmatic of the conflict between development and displacement.
paradox: A seemingly absurd statement that when investigated may prove to be true

paragon: A person or thing regarded as a perfect example of a particular quality
Mahatma Gandhi is considered a paragon of non-violent resistance.
pariah: A person or animal that is generally despised or avoided; an outcast
Nations that violate international law risk becoming pariahs in the global community.
parochial: Having a limited or narrow outlook; relating to a parish
Parochial politics that ignores national interest cannot serve the country well.
parsimonious: Unwilling to spend money or use resources; frugal
The parsimonious budget allocation affected public services.
parsimony: Extreme unwillingness to spend money; extreme frugality
The government's parsimony in health expenditure has cost lives during the pandemic.
pathological: Compulsive; unreasonable; relating to or caused by disease
A pathological inability to accept criticism led to his political downfall.
patronise: To treat someone with apparent kindness but in a condescending way; to frequent
Development programmes that patronise rural communities fail to empower them.
pedantic: Excessively concerned with minor details or rules; overscrupulous
The pedantic teacher focused on trivial grammatical errors.
pedantry: Excessive concern with minor details or rules; slavish adherence to formality
The pedantry of procedural requirements prevented urgent relief from reaching flood victims.
pejoration: The process by which a word's meaning becomes more negative over time
The word 'politician' has undergone pejoration in public discourse over recent decades.
pejorative: Expressing contempt or disapproval; having negative connotations
Using pejorative terms in public discourse damages civil dialogue.
pellucid: Translucently clear; easily understood; expressing clearly
The judge's pellucid reasoning left no room for ambiguity in interpretation.
penchant: A strong or habitual liking for something or tendency to do something
The author has a penchant for historical fiction.
penitent: Feeling or showing sorrow for wrongdoing; repentant
The penitent accused sought leniency from the court, citing genuine remorse.
penultimate: Second to last in a series
The penultimate round of negotiations was the most difficult before the treaty was signed.
penury: Penury drove thousands of drought-affected farmers to seek daily wage work in cities.
peremptory: Insisting on immediate attention; not open to appeal or question
The court issued a peremptory order directing the state to act within 48 hours.
perennial: Lasting or existing for a long or apparently infinite time; enduring
Unemployment remains a perennial problem in developing economies.
perfidious: Deceitful and untrustworthy; guilty of betrayal
The perfidious ally defected to the opposing camp on the eve of the parliamentary vote.
perfidy: The spy's perfidy resulted in countless casualties.
perfunctory: Carried out with minimum effort or reflection
The inspector's perfunctory examination missed critical safety issues.
peripheral: Relating to or situated on the edge or periphery; of secondary importance
The report mentioned only peripheral issues without addressing core problems.
perjury: The offence of wilfully telling an untruth in a court of law
The witness was charged with perjury after his testimony was contradicted by CCTV footage.
pernicious: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way
Misinformation can have a pernicious effect on democracy.
perspicacious: Having a ready insight into and understanding of things; mentally sharp
The perspicacious analyst predicted the market crash months in advance.
perspicacity: A ready insight into things; shrewdness
The negotiator's perspicacity in reading the other side's position gave India a decisive edge.
pertinacious: Holding firmly to an opinion or course of action; persistent
The pertinacious activist filed PIL after PIL until the illegal mine was shut down.
pertinent: Relevant or applicable to a particular matter; appropriate
The lawyer raised several pertinent questions during cross-examination.
perturbation: Anxiety or mental uneasiness; a deviation from a regular state

pervasive: Spreading widely throughout an area or group of people
Corruption became pervasive in the administrative system.
petulance: The quality of being petulant; childish sulkiness
The minister's petulance when questioned by journalists projected poorly on national television.
petulant: The petulant response from the official surprised journalists.
philanthropic: Seeking to promote the welfare of others, especially by donating money to good causes
The industrialist's philanthropic efforts built numerous hospitals.
philanthropist: A person who seeks to promote the welfare of others, especially by donating money
The industrialist became a celebrated philanthropist after endowing rural hospitals.
phlegmatic: Having a calm and unemotional disposition; not easily excited
The phlegmatic judge remained unmoved by the dramatic theatrics of both counsels.
pious: Devoutly religious; making a show of virtue; sanctimonious

piquant: Having a pleasantly sharp and appetising flavour; agreeably stimulating
The piquant commentary of the veteran journalist added colour to otherwise dry proceedings.
placate: To make someone less angry or hostile; to appease
The government's token measures failed to placate the agitating farmers.
plaintive: The plaintive cries of displaced families moved even the most hardened relief workers.
platitude: A remark or statement that has been used too often to be interesting or thoughtful
The speech was full of platitudes rather than concrete solutions.
platitudinous: Using or full of dull, unoriginal remarks
The platitudinous inaugural address failed to inspire the graduating students.
plethora: A large or excessive amount of something

pluralism: The existence of a variety of groups or parties within a state
India's constitutional commitment to pluralism is its greatest democratic achievement.
poignant: Evoking a keen sense of sadness or regret; deeply moving

polemic: A strong verbal or written attack on someone or something

polity: A form of government or constitution; an organised society

ponderous: Slow and clumsy because of great weight; laborious

pontificate: To express opinions in a pompous way; to act as a pontiff

portentous: Of or like a portent; giving a sign or warning that something momentous is likely to happen
The portentous silence before the storm made everyone nervous.
posit: To put forward as a fact or as a basis for argument; to assume

pragmatic: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically based on practical considerations
The government adopted a pragmatic approach to foreign policy.
precipitate: To cause something to happen suddenly or unexpectedly; hasty or rash

precipitous: Dangerously high or steep; done suddenly and without careful consideration
The precipitous decline in foreign reserves triggered emergency borrowing from the IMF.
preclude: To prevent from happening; make impossible

predilection: A preference or special liking for something; a bias

preeminent: Surpassing all others; very distinguished in some way

prerogative: A right or privilege exclusive to a particular individual or class
The president exercised his prerogative to grant pardons.
prescient: Having or showing knowledge of events before they take place
The economist's prescient warnings about the crisis were ignored.
presumptuous: Failing to observe the limits of what is permitted or appropriate

prevail: To prove more powerful or superior; to be widespread

prevaricate: To speak or act evasively; to avoid the truth

probative: Having the quality of proving or demonstrating something; providing proof
The court admitted only probative evidence that directly bore on the guilt of the accused.
probity: The quality of having strong moral principles; complete honesty
Public servants of probity are the backbone of a trustworthy administration.
proclivity: A tendency to choose or do something regularly; an inclination
His proclivity for populist announcements over structural reforms alarmed economists.
procrastinate: To delay or postpone action; put off doing something
Students often procrastinate on assignments until the deadline approaches.
prodigal: Spending money or resources freely and recklessly; wastefully extravagant
The prodigal heir squandered the family fortune within years.
prodigious: Remarkably or impressively great in extent, size, or degree
The athlete showed prodigious talent from a young age.
profligate: The profligate spending of public funds on luxury items drew audit objections.
profound: Very great or intense; showing deep insight or understanding
The philosopher's profound insights influenced generations of thinkers.
proliferate: To increase rapidly in numbers; multiply

promulgate: To promote or make widely known an idea or cause; to put a law into effect
The government promulgated new environmental regulations.
propensity: An inclination or natural tendency to behave in a particular way
Humans have a propensity for pattern recognition.
propitious: Giving or indicating a good chance of success; favorable

prosaic: Lacking poetic beauty; commonplace or dull
The writer's prosaic style failed to engage readers.
proscribe: To forbid, especially by law; to denounce or condemn

protean: Tending or able to change frequently or easily; versatile
The actor's protean talent allowed him to play diverse roles.
provincial: Of or concerning a province; unsophisticated or narrow-minded

prudent: Acting with or showing care and thought for the future
Prudent financial planning ensures long-term stability.
punctilious: Showing great attention to detail or correct behavior
The punctilious accountant never missed a single entry.
quagmire: A soft boggy area; a difficult situation from which it is hard to escape
The government found itself in a diplomatic quagmire after contradictory statements by two ministers.
quandary: A state of perplexity or uncertainty over what to do in a difficult situation
The judge faced a quandary between justice and mercy.
querulous: Complaining in a petulant or whining manner
The querulous customer complained about every minor issue.
quixotic: The quixotic plan to end poverty overnight was quickly dismissed.
quotidian: Of or occurring every day; ordinary or everyday
The quotidian struggles of migrant workers rarely make headlines.
raconteur: A person who tells anecdotes in a skilful and amusing way
The retired diplomat was a celebrated raconteur whose stories illuminated decades of foreign policy.
ramification: A complex or unwelcome consequence of an action
The ramifications of the demonetisation policy are still being debated by economists.
rancorous: The rancorous debate damaged relationships between coalition partners.
rapacious: Rapacious mining companies stripped the tribal lands of their natural wealth.
ratiocinate: To reason or argue in a logical manner
The judge was known to ratiocinate carefully before delivering landmark constitutional judgements.
ratiocination: Sound judicial decisions require careful ratiocination, not emotional responses.
rebuke: To express sharp disapproval of someone because of their behaviour
The Supreme Court rebuked the state government for contemptuous disregard of its earlier orders.
recalcitrant: The recalcitrant state refused to implement central directives.
recapitulate: To summarise and restate the main points of something

recidivism: The tendency to relapse into criminal behaviour

reclusive: Avoiding the company of other people; solitary
The reclusive author rarely appeared in public.
recondite: Little known; abstruse; dealing with very profound subjects

recrimination: An accusation in response to one from someone else; counter-charge

rectitude: The appointment committee placed rectitude above political loyalty in selecting the CBI director.
redolent: Strongly reminiscent or suggestive of something
The old house was redolent of memories from childhood.
redundant: Not or no longer needed or useful; superfluous
The additional security measures seemed redundant after careful analysis.
refractory: Stubborn or unmanageable; resistant to treatment or change

refulgent: The refulgent achievements of ISRO have lit up India's global scientific standing.
refutation: The action of proving a statement or theory to be wrong
The defence counsel offered a compelling refutation of the prosecution's circumstantial evidence.
refute: To prove a statement or theory to be wrong or false; disprove
Scientists refuted the conspiracy theory with empirical evidence.
rejoinder: A sharp or witty reply; a response to a reply
The defence counsel's rejoinder to the prosecution's closing argument was devastatingly precise.
relegate: To consign or dismiss to an inferior rank or position
The once-powerful minister was relegated to a minor department.
relinquish: To voluntarily cease to keep or claim; to give up
The officer refused to relinquish his integrity despite offers of a lucrative posting.
remiss: Lacking care or attention to duty; negligent

remonstrance: A forcefully reproachful protest; a formal complaint

remonstrate: To make a forcefully reproachful protest
Civil society organisations remonstrated against the dilution of environmental clearance norms.
renounce: To formally declare one's abandonment of a claim, right, or possession
The leader renounced violence and adopted peaceful methods.
replete: The museum is replete with rare artifacts from ancient civilizations.
reprehensible: Deserving censure or condemnation; morally wrong
The corrupt official's reprehensible conduct shocked the public.
reprieve: A cancellation or postponement of punishment; temporary relief
The governor granted a last-minute reprieve to the convict.
reprisal: The government warned that any attack on its forces would invite swift reprisals.
reproach: To express disapproval of someone; criticism or blame
The Prime Minister faced reproach from the opposition for bypassing Parliament on key decisions.
reprobate: The system protected reprobates while punishing honest whistleblowers.
repudiate: To refuse to accept or be associated with; to deny the truth
The party repudiated the extremist views of some members.
repudiation: The refusal to accept or be associated with something
The government's repudiation of the treaty angered its allies and damaged diplomatic relations.
rescind: To revoke, cancel, or repeal a law, order, or agreement

rescission: The revocation, cancellation, or repeal of a law, order, or agreement
The rescission of the farm laws followed months of sustained protest.
resilience: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties; toughness

resolute: The resolute leader pursued reforms despite strong opposition.
resonant: Producing a deep, clear sound; evoking a strong emotional response
Nehru's words about India's tryst with destiny remain resonant across generations.
reticence: The quality of being reserved about one's thoughts or feelings
The bureaucrat's reticence under questioning was interpreted as evasion by the inquiry committee.
reticent: Not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily; reserved
The reticent witness provided minimal information to investigators.
retrenchment: The reduction of costs by cutting staff or spending; a defensive policy
The fiscal deficit triggered a period of severe retrenchment in public expenditure.
rhetoric: The art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing

sacrosanct: Regarded as too important or valuable to be interfered with
The independence of the judiciary is sacrosanct in a constitutional democracy.
sagacious: Having or showing keen mental discernment and good judgment
The sagacious leader foresaw the economic challenges ahead.
sagacity: The quality of being sagacious; wisdom and good judgment
The elder's sagacity helped resolve the community dispute.
salient: The report highlighted the salient features of the new policy.
salubrious: The salubrious mountain air helped patients recover faster.
sanctimonious: Making a show of being morally superior; self-righteous

sanction: Official permission or approval; a penalty for disobeying

sanguine: Optimistic or positive, especially in difficult situations

sardonic: Grimly mocking or cynical; scornfully derisive

saturnine: Slow and gloomy; having a forbidding or sullen appearance

scathing: The CAG released a scathing report on the mismanagement of public funds.
scepticism: A doubting attitude; the doctrine that true knowledge is unattainable

scintillating: Sparkling or shining brightly; brilliantly and excitingly clever

scrupulous: Very concerned to avoid doing wrong; careful and thorough

scrutinize: To examine or inspect closely and thoroughly

scurrilous: Making or spreading scandalous claims about someone; grossly offensive

secular: Not connected with religious or spiritual matters; worldly

sedition: Conduct or speech inciting rebellion against the authority of a state

sedulous: The sedulous researcher spent years perfecting the experiment.
sedulousness: The quality of showing dedication and diligence

serendipitous: Occurring by chance in a happy or beneficial way
The serendipitous discovery of the ancient inscription rewrote the history of the region.
serendipity: The occurrence of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way
The discovery of penicillin was a classic example of serendipity in scientific research.
servile: Having or showing an excessive willingness to serve or please others
The servile behavior toward superiors was considered undignified.
shibboleth: A custom, phrase, or practice that distinguishes one group from another

sinister: Giving the impression that something harmful or evil is happening

skeptical: Not easily convinced; having doubts or reservations

solecism: A grammatical error; a breach of etiquette or social norms

solemn: Formal and dignified; characterized by deep sincerity

solicitous: Characterized by concern or attention; eager to help

solvent: Having assets in excess of liabilities; able to dissolve other substances

somber: Dark or dull in color or tone; oppressively solemn or sober

sophistry: The use of clever but false arguments; specious reasoning

sophomoric: Pretentious or juvenile; overconfident of knowledge but poorly informed
The politician's sophomoric understanding of economics was evident.
soporific: The minister's soporific budget speech had several members nodding off in the House.
specious: Superficially plausible but actually wrong; misleading in appearance
The lawyer's specious arguments failed to convince the jury.
speculative: Based on theory or conjecture rather than firm evidence; engaged in risky financial deals
The report's speculative claims about economic growth were challenged by the RBI.
spurious: Not being what it purports to be; false or fake
The expert identified the painting as a spurious copy.
squander: To waste something, especially money or time, in a reckless manner

stalwart: Loyal, reliable, and hardworking; strongly built

steadfast: Resolutely or dutifully firm and unwavering

steadfastness: The quality of being resolutely firm and unwavering

stentorian: The judge's stentorian voice silenced the courtroom instantly.
stigmatise: To describe or regard as worthy of disgrace or disapproval

stoic: Enduring pain and hardship without showing feelings or complaining
The soldier remained stoic despite severe injuries.
stolid: Calm and dependable; showing little emotion

stolidity: The quality of being calm and not easily moved

stringent: Stringent environmental regulations protect natural resources.
stymie: To prevent or hinder the progress of something

subjective: Based on personal feelings rather than external facts

subjugate: To bring under domination or control, especially by conquest

sublime: Of such excellence, grandeur, or beauty as to inspire great admiration

subordinate: Lower in rank or position; treating as of lesser importance

subservient: Obsequiously servile; less important; serving as a means to an end

substantiate: To provide evidence to support or prove the truth of

substantiation: The action of providing evidence to prove the truth of something

substantive: Having a firm basis in reality; significant and meaningful

subterfuge: Deceit used in order to achieve one's goal

subtle: So delicate or precise as to be difficult to analyze or describe

subversive: Seeking to undermine or overthrow an established system or institution

succinct: The executive summary provided a succinct overview of findings.
suffrage: The right to vote in political elections

supercilious: Behaving as if one is superior to others; contemptuous

superficial: Existing or occurring at or on the surface; lacking depth

superfluous: Unnecessary, especially through being more than enough

supine: Lying face upwards; failing to act due to laziness or weakness

supplant: To supersede and replace; to take the place of

supplication: The action of asking or begging for something humbly

suppress: To forcibly put an end to; to prevent from being seen or known

surmise: To suppose something is true without having evidence to confirm it

surreptitious: Kept secret, especially because it would not be approved of

surreptitiousness: The quality of doing something secretly or stealthily

sybaritic: Fond of luxury and pleasure; relating to a sybarite

sycophancy: Obsequious behaviour toward someone important; excessive flattery

sycophant: A person who acts obsequiously toward someone important to gain advantage

symbiosis: A mutually beneficial relationship between two parties

synergy: The interaction of two or more agents to produce a combined effect greater than the sum of their parts

synthesis: The combination of components to form a connected whole

systemic: Relating to a whole system rather than particular parts

taciturn: Reserved or uncommunicative in speech; saying little

taciturnity: The habit of being reserved and saying little; uncommunicativeness
The judge's taciturnity during arguments unnerved even experienced advocates.
tangential: Diverging from a previous course; merely touching a matter peripherally

tantamount: Equivalent in seriousness to; virtually the same as

tautology: Saying the same thing twice in different words; needless repetition

temerity: The junior officer had the temerity to challenge the minister's decision publicly.
temperate: Showing moderation or self-restraint; relating to moderate climate

temporal: Relating to worldly as opposed to spiritual affairs; relating to time

tenable: Able to be maintained or defended against attack or objection

tenacious: Not readily relinquishing a position, principle, or course of action; determined

tenacity: The quality of being tenacious; persistent determination

tendentious: Promoting a particular cause or point of view; biased

tendril: A slender thread-like appendage; something that grasps or entwines

tenebrous: The tenebrous alleyways of the old city concealed a network of illicit trade.
tentative: Not certain or fixed; provisional; hesitant or uncertain

terse: Sparing in the use of words; abrupt

timorous: Showing or suffering from nervousness or lack of confidence

tirade: A long, angry speech of criticism or accusation

toady: A person who flatters and defers to others in the hope of gain; a sycophant

torpor: A state of physical or mental inactivity; lethargy

torturous: Full of twists and turns; causing great pain or suffering

tractable: Easy to control or influence; manageable

tranquil: Free from disturbance; calm and peaceful

transcend: To go beyond the range or limits of; to surpass

transgress: To go beyond the limits of what is morally or legally acceptable

transgression: An act that goes against a law or moral code; an offence

transient: Lasting only for a short time; temporary

treachery: The general's treachery in leaking battle plans to the enemy cost hundreds of lives.
trenchant: Vigorous or incisive in expression; cutting

trepidation: A feeling of fear or anxiety about something that may happen

trite: Overused and consequently lacking in interest or originality

truculent: Eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant

truncate: To shorten by cutting off the top or end

tumultuous: Making a loud, confused noise; uproarious or characterized by disorder

turbulent: Characterized by conflict, disorder, or confusion; not stable

turpitude: Depravity; wickedness; a depraved or wicked act

tyrannical: Exercising power in a cruel and arbitrary way

tyranny: Cruel and oppressive government or rule; oppressive power

ubiquitous: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society.
ubiquity: The fact of appearing everywhere or of being very common

ukase: An arbitrary or peremptory command; an edict

umbrage: Offence or annoyance; shade from trees

umbral: Relating to shadow or shade; deeply shaded

unassailable: Unable to be attacked or questioned; beyond criticism

unassuming: Despite great achievements, the scientist remained unassuming.
unconscionable: Not right or reasonable; shockingly unfair or unjust

unctuous: The unctuous behavior of the courtier disgusted observers.
undermine: To erode the base or foundation; to weaken or damage gradually

undulate: To move with a smooth wavelike motion; to have a wavy form

unequivocal: Leaving no doubt; unambiguous and clear

unfettered: Democracy ensures unfettered freedom of expression.
uniformity: The quality or state of being uniform; consistency

unilateral: Performed by or affecting only one person, group, or country

unimpeachable: Not able to be doubted, questioned, or criticized; entirely trustworthy

unprecedented: Never done or known before; without previous example

unscrupulous: Having or showing no moral principles; not honest or fair
untenable: Not able to be maintained or defended against attack

unwavering: The leader showed unwavering commitment to reform.
urbane: Suave, courteous, and refined in manner

usurp: To take a position of power or importance illegally or by force

usurpation: The action of taking a position of power by force or without legal right

utilitarian: Designed to be useful or practical rather than attractive

utopian: Modeled on or aiming for a state in which everything is perfect; idealistic

vacillate: To alternate or waver between different opinions or actions

vacillation: The inability to make up one's mind; wavering between options

vacuous: Having or showing a lack of thought or intelligence; empty

vague: Of uncertain, indefinite, or unclear character or meaning

vainglory: Inordinate pride in oneself or one's achievements; empty boasting

vapid: Offering nothing that is stimulating or challenging; insipid

vehement: Showing strong feeling; forceful, passionate, or intense

venal: Showing or motivated by susceptibility to bribery; corrupt

vendetta: A prolonged bitter quarrel with or campaign against someone

venerable: Accorded a great deal of respect, especially because of age or wisdom

venerate: To regard with great respect; to revere

veracious: Speaking or representing the truth; truthful

veracity: Conformity to facts; accuracy and truthfulness

verbiage: The legal notice was buried under so much verbiage that its core demand was unclear.
verbose: Using or expressed in more words than are needed

vex: To make someone feel annoyed, frustrated, or worried

viable: Solar energy presents a viable alternative to fossil fuels.
vicarious: Experienced in the imagination through the feelings or actions of another

vicissitude: A change of circumstances; alternation of fortune

vigilant: Keeping careful watch for possible danger or difficulties

vindicate: To clear someone of blame or suspicion; to show to be right

vindictive: Having or showing a strong desire for revenge
virtuoso: A person highly skilled in music or another artistic pursuit

virulent: Extremely severe or harmful in its effects; bitterly hostile

visceral: Relating to deep inward feelings rather than the intellect

vitiate: To impair the quality or efficiency of something; to make legally invalid

vitriolic: Filled with bitter criticism or malice; caustic

vituperate: To blame or insult someone in strong or violent language

vivacious: The vivacious speaker energized the conference audience.
vociferous: Expressing or characterized by vehement opinions; loud and forceful

volatile: Liable to change rapidly and unpredictably; explosive

volition: The faculty or power of using one's will; conscious choice

vulnerable: Susceptible to physical or emotional attack or harm

wane: To decrease in vigor, power, or extent; to decline

wanton: The court condemned the wanton destruction of public property.
wary: Feeling or showing caution about possible dangers or problems

whimsical: Playfully quaint or fanciful, especially in an appealing way

wield: To hold and use something as a weapon or tool; to have power

willful: Intentional; deliberate; having a stubborn determination

winsome: Attractive or appealing in appearance or character

wistful: Having or showing a feeling of vague or regretful longing

wizened: The wizened sage shared wisdom gained over decades.

wrath: The people faced the wrath of nature during the devastating flood.
wretched: In a very unhappy or unfortunate state; of poor quality

xenophilia: Love of or attraction to foreign people and cultures

xenophobia: Dislike of or prejudice against people from other countries
yearn: To have an intense feeling of longing for something

yeoman: A man holding a small landed estate; someone who does good, loyal service

yielding: Giving way under pressure; compliant or submissive

zeal: Great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit of a cause or objective

zealous: Having or showing great energy or enthusiasm for a cause

galling: Annoying or humiliating to an extreme degree

gerrymander: To manipulate electoral boundaries for political advantage

gumption: Shrewd or spirited initiative and resourcefulness

gustatory: Relating to the sense of taste

halcyon: Denoting a period of time that was idyllically happy and peaceful

hamstring: To severely restrict the efficiency or effectiveness of someone

haughtiness: The quality of being arrogantly superior and disdainful

hedonism: The pursuit of pleasure as the highest good and chief aim of life

hegira: A journey to a more desirable place; a mass departure or exodus

hermetic: Completely sealed; airtight; relating to esoteric knowledge

heterodox: Not conforming to accepted or orthodox standards or beliefs

hidebound: Unwilling to change because of tradition or convention

hortatory: Tending or aiming to exhort; encouraging or urging strongly

hue: A colour or shade; a character or aspect of something

kindred: Similar in kind; related by blood or sharing qualities

kinetic: Relating to or resulting from motion; energetic and dynamic

knavery: The knavery of the middlemen was exposed when farmers received only a fraction of the market price.

hypothetical: Based on an assumed scenario rather than fact; supposed

kvetch: To complain persistently and in a whining manner

lackey: A servant or follower who obeys without question

larceny: The auditor uncovered grand larceny disguised as routine procurement expenses.
largesse: Generosity in bestowing money or gifts upon others

legerdemain: Skilful use of hands in tricks; deception or trickery

libertine: A person who behaves without moral principles especially in sexual matters

limpid: Completely clear and transparent; easy to understand

neophyte: A person who is new to a subject, skill, or belief

nettle: To irritate or annoy someone; a plant with stinging hairs

obeisance: Deferential respect; a bow or curtsy as a gesture of respect

waive: To refrain from insisting on or using a right or claim; to forgo

wraith: A ghost or ghostlike image of someone, especially seen before or after death

yore: Long ago; time past; former times

adage: A proverb or short statement expressing a general truth
The old adage about prevention being better than cure applies perfectly to public health policy.
albeit: The economy grew, albeit at a slower pace than projected.
allegory: A story or picture with a hidden meaning, typically moral or political
George Orwell's Animal Farm is an allegory of the Russian Revolution.
anathema: Something or someone that one vehemently dislikes; a formal curse

ancillary: Providing necessary support to primary activities of an organisation
The hospital lacked ancillary services such as pathology and radiology.
animus: Hostility or ill feeling; motivation to do something

antediluvian: Of or belonging to time before the biblical Flood; ridiculously old-fashioned

apathy: Lack of interest, enthusiasm, or concern for things

aplomb: Self-confidence or assurance especially in a demanding situation
The young diplomat handled the press conference with remarkable aplomb.
baneful: Causing great distress or annoyance; harmful and destructive

bemused: Puzzled, confused, or bewildered by something

besmirch: To damage the reputation of someone; to make dirty or stained
The opposition tried to besmirch the minister's reputation with unverified allegations.
bilious: Affected by nausea; bad-tempered or spiteful in manner

bivouac: A temporary camp without tents or cover used by soldiers or travellers
The army set up a bivouac along the border in response to the security alert.
bluster: To talk in a loud aggressive way with little effect; empty threats
Behind the diplomatic bluster lay a genuine fear of economic sanctions.
bohemian: A person who lives an unconventional artistic lifestyle
The bohemian culture of Calcutta's coffeehouses shaped Bengal's intellectual movements.
bromide: A trite and unoriginal remark intended to soothe; a commonplace statement
The minister's speech was full of bromides about national unity without concrete proposals.
bumptious: Self-assertive or proud to an irritating degree; conceited
The bumptious new officer alienated senior colleagues with his overbearing attitude.
cadre: A small group of trained people forming the core of a larger organisation

calumniate: To make false and defamatory statements about someone

canker: A disease of plants; a corrupting or demoralising influence that spreads
Nepotism is a canker that destroys institutional merit and public trust.
chasten: To have a restraining or moderating effect; to discipline through suffering
The electoral defeat chastened the party into deep introspection and reform.
clarion: Loud and clear; a clear ringing sound or urgent call to action
The editorial was a clarion call for judicial reform across the nation.
cognoscenti: People who are especially well informed about a particular subject
The cognoscenti of classical music gathered for the annual festival in Chennai.
colloquy: A formal conversation or dialogue between parties
The chief justices held a colloquy on judicial reforms at the national conference.
confluence: The junction of two rivers; a coming together of people or things
The confluence of demographic dividend and digital infrastructure offers India a unique opportunity.
connivance: Willingness to secretly allow or assist wrongdoing; tacit approval
The scam could not have succeeded without the connivance of senior officials.
consanguinity: Descent from the same ancestor; blood relationship between people
Laws prohibiting consanguinity in marriage aim to prevent genetic disorders.
constellation: A group of stars forming a pattern; a group of associated people or things

contravention: An action that violates a law, treaty, or agreement
The construction was undertaken in contravention of environmental clearance norms.
convalescence: The gradual recovery of health and strength after illness or injury
The economy showed signs of convalescence after two quarters of contraction.
corrigendum: A correction to be made in a printed text; an erratum
The ministry issued a corrigendum to the earlier notification clarifying eligibility criteria.
coterie: A small group of people with shared interests or tastes; an exclusive circle

countermand: To revoke or cancel an order previously given
The general countermanded the earlier order when intelligence revealed an ambush.
cronyism: The appointment of friends to positions of authority without regard to qualifications

curmudgeon: A bad-tempered or surly person; a grumpy individual
The veteran parliamentarian was known as a loveable curmudgeon who spoke his mind.
cynosure: A person or thing that is the centre of attention or admiration
The young IPS officer became the cynosure of the community for her anti-corruption drive.
dalliance: A casual romantic relationship; a brief involvement with something

dastardly: Wicked and cruel; cowardly and contemptible in nature

declaim: To speak or recite in a rhetorical impassioned way; to orate
The leader declaimed against corruption from the public platform with fiery eloquence.
defray: To provide money to pay a cost or expense for someone
The scholarship will defray the cost of tuition for students from rural backgrounds.
demography: The study of populations, their size, structure, and distribution
India's demography presents both a dividend and a challenge for policymakers.
derisive: Expressing contempt or ridicule; mocking in tone
The opposition's derisive laughter during the minister's speech disrupted proceedings.
descry: To catch sight of something; to discern or detect from a distance

devolve: To transfer power or responsibility to a lower level of authority

dialectic: The art of investigating truth through logical argumentation and discussion
The dialectic between tradition and modernity defines contemporary Indian society.
diminution: A reduction in size, extent, or importance of something
Any diminution of judicial independence threatens the constitutional framework.
dint: Force or effort; by means of persistent effort
By dint of relentless effort, the district achieved full literacy ahead of schedule.
disconsolate: Without consolation or comfort; unhappy beyond any comforting
The disconsolate farmers watched as unseasonal rains destroyed their standing crops.
discrepancy: A lack of compatibility or similarity between two or more facts
The discrepancy between declared income and lifestyle triggered a tax investigation.
dissolution: The closing down of an assembly; the process of dissolving or ending
The dissolution of the state assembly led to fresh elections within six months.
dossier: A collection of documents about a particular person, event, or subject
Intelligence agencies prepared a detailed dossier on cross-border infiltration routes.
draconian: Excessively harsh and severe especially of laws or punishments
The draconian sedition law has been criticised for suppressing legitimate dissent.
dross: Something regarded as worthless; waste matter or impurities
The editorial urged readers to separate the dross of propaganda from genuine journalism.
duress: Threats, violence, or constraint used to force someone to act against their will
The confession obtained under duress was declared inadmissible by the High Court.
dystopia: An imagined state or society where there is great suffering or injustice
The surveillance state depicted in the novel served as a warning against technological dystopia.
xenophile: A person who is attracted to foreign cultures, peoples, and customs
India's first Prime Minister was a xenophile who admired Western democratic institutions while championing Indian identity.
xerophyte: A plant adapted to survive in dry or arid conditions
Rajasthan's xerophytes like cacti and thorny bushes form the backbone of desert ecology.
xenogenous: Caused by an external agent; originating from outside the organism
The committee investigated whether the economic disruption was xenogenous or caused by domestic policy failure.
xenomania: An excessive or unreasonable fondness for foreign things

yearning: A feeling of intense longing for something lost or absent

yokel: An unsophisticated person from a rural area
The urban elite's dismissal of rural voters as yokels reflects a dangerous disconnect from grassroots democracy.
yonder: At some distance in the direction indicated; over there
The promised land of development lies yonder for aspirational districts identified under NITI Aayog.
zilch: Despite crores allocated, the rural electrification scheme delivered zilch in the remotest hamlets.
jurisprudence: The theory or philosophy of law; the study of legal systems and principles
Indian jurisprudence draws from the Constitution, common law, and ancient texts like Arthashastra.
jibe: To be in accord or agree; a taunting remark

jeopardise: To put something or someone into a situation where there is danger of loss or harm

jocund: The jocund atmosphere at the village fair contrasted sharply with the drought-stricken landscape.
jaundiced: Having a prejudiced or cynical outlook influenced by bitterness or envy

jingoist: A person who strongly believes their country should use military force against others

jovial: The jovial demeanour of the district magistrate put nervous candidates at ease during interviews.
jeopardous: Involving or exposing one to danger; hazardous and risky
The jeopardous trek through Siachen remains one of the most demanding military deployments in the world.
yeomanry: A body of yeomen; loyal and hardworking service class
The yeomanry of India's civil services forms the administrative backbone of governance.
yawn: A wide opening; a tediously dull or boring event or situation

junket: An extravagant trip or celebration at public expense

kafkaesque: Oppressively complex and nightmarish in a manner associated with Franz Kafka's fiction

keynesian: Relating to the economic theories of John Maynard Keynes advocating government intervention
India adopted Keynesian stimulus measures during the 2020 pandemic to revive economic demand.
kismet: Destiny or fate; the will of God or the universe

kudzu: A rapidly growing invasive plant; metaphorically any problem that spreads uncontrollably

kerygma: The preaching of the Gospel; the essential message or proclamation
Every political manifesto carries its own kerygma aimed at converting undecided voters.
kith: One's friends, acquaintances, and relations collectively

kakistocracy: Government by the least qualified or most unprincipled citizens

kaleidoscopic: Having complex patterns of colours; continually shifting and changing
India's kaleidoscopic cultural diversity is both its greatest strength and governance challenge.
quaff: To drink something heartily and with great enjoyment
The victorious cricket team quaffed from the trophy in celebration after the World Cup win.
quash: To reject or void especially by legal procedure; to suppress forcefully

quasi: Seemingly or apparently but not really; having a resemblance to

quizzical: Indicating mild or amused puzzlement; questioning and slightly mocking
The auditor gave a quizzical look at the accounts that showed zero expenditure on a completed project.
feckless: Lacking initiative or strength of character; irresponsible and incompetent

febrile: Relating to or affected by fever; marked by nervous excitement

firebrand: A person who is passionate about a particular cause and often incites others to action

fulcrum: The point on which a lever rests or pivots; a thing that plays a central role

fracas: A fracas erupted in the state assembly when the ruling party tabled the controversial resolution.
fortitude: Courage in pain or adversity; mental and emotional strength
The fortitude shown by CRPF jawans in hostile terrain exemplifies the spirit of national service.
forebode: To act as a warning or indication of a future event, especially a bad one

gauche: Lacking ease or grace; unsophisticated and socially awkward

gestalt: An organised whole that is perceived as more than the sum of its parts

guileful: Deviously clever and cunning; full of treachery

guffaw: The veteran parliamentarian's witty retort drew guffaws even from the opposition benches.
nabob: A person of conspicuous wealth or high status, especially one who acquired riches in India

nacreous: Having a pearly lustre or sheen; resembling mother-of-pearl
The nacreous clouds over the Himalayas created a spectacular visual phenomenon for meteorologists.
nativity: The occasion of a person's birth; the circumstances of origin

nonpareil: Having no equal; unrivalled and matchless in quality

nugatory: Having no purpose or value; useless and futile

underwrite: To accept financial responsibility for; to guarantee or support an undertaking

usurious: Relating to the practice of lending money at unreasonably high rates of interest

windfall: An unexpected and large gain or piece of good fortune

histrionics: Exaggerated dramatic behaviour designed to attract attention

hullabaloo: A commotion or uproar; a great fuss about something

niggardly: Not generous; stingy and reluctant to spend or give

nimble: Quick and light in movement or action; agile in thinking

nondescript: Lacking distinctive or interesting features or characteristics
The nondescript building housed one of India's most sensitive intelligence operations.
numismatic: Relating to the study or collection of coins, banknotes, and medals
The numismatic evidence from Mauryan-era coins confirms the extent of Ashoka's empire across the subcontinent.
nuptial: The nuptial customs across India's diverse communities reflect its rich cultural pluralism.
ulterior: Existing beyond what is obvious or admitted; hidden and undisclosed

uxorious: Having or showing an excessive fondness for one's wife
Critics called the king uxorious, claiming his wife's influence distorted court policy.
waif: A homeless and helpless person, especially a neglected child
The waifs living under railway platforms in Mumbai inspired the plot of the Oscar-winning film.
heuristic: Enabling a person to discover or learn something for themselves through practical experience
The National Education Policy promotes heuristic learning methods over rote memorisation.
hieratic: Relating to priests or the priestly class; highly stylised and formal

hyperbolic: Deliberately exaggerated; using hyperbole for emphasis

hinterland: The remote areas of a country away from the coast or major cities; the area around or beyond a major town

ecclesiastical: Relating to the Christian Church or its clergy

edification: The instruction or improvement of a person morally or intellectually

elysian: Relating to or resembling paradise; blissful and divine

emissary: A person sent on a special mission usually as a diplomatic representative

enamour: To be filled with a feeling of love or deep admiration for someone or something

entropy: A gradual decline into disorder; a measure of disorder in a system

erstwhile: The erstwhile princely states were integrated into the Indian Union through Sardar Patel's diplomacy.
ethos: The characteristic spirit or attitudes of a community or era

eulogy: A speech or piece of writing that praises someone highly, especially one given at a funeral

expatriate: A person who lives outside their native country

litany: A tedious recital or repetitive series; a form of prayer with repeated responses

lithe: Thin, supple, and graceful in movement

limpet: A person or thing that clings tenaciously; a type of marine mollusc that grips rocks tightly

lumber: To move in a slow heavy awkward way; to burden someone with unwanted responsibilities

matriculate: To be enrolled at a college or university; to formally register as a student

milieu: A person's social environment; the physical or social setting in which something occurs

miscegenation: The interbreeding of people considered to be of different racial groups

moratorium: A temporary prohibition or suspension of an activity

motley: Incongruously varied in appearance or character; disparate and diverse

mundane: Lacking interest or excitement; dull and ordinary in everyday life

tableau: A vivid or graphic description; a group of people arranged to represent a scene

talisman: An object thought to have magic powers and bring good luck

tawdry: Showy but cheap and of poor quality; morally distasteful

theocracy: A system of government in which priests or religious leaders rule in the name of God

travesty: A false, absurd, or distorted representation of something

torpid: Mentally or physically inactive; sluggish and lethargic

tome: A large heavy scholarly book or volume

tumult: A loud confused noise from a large crowd; confusion or disorder

rapprochement: An establishment or resumption of harmonious relations between previously hostile parties

ravenous: Extremely hungry; having a voracious appetite for something

recumbent: Lying down in a comfortable position; reclining

redoubtable: Formidable, especially as an opponent; commanding respect through age or achievement

remuneration: Money paid for work or a service; compensation for professional effort

reprobation: Strong disapproval or condemnation; the act of expressing severe censure

revanchism: A policy of seeking to retaliate or recover lost territory

rife: Widespread and common, especially of something unpleasant

rubric: A set of rules or instructions; a heading or category for classification

rusticate: To suspend a student from university as punishment; to live or go to the countryside

sacrilege: Violation or misuse of something regarded as sacred

salvo: A simultaneous discharge of weapons; a sudden series of aggressive statements or acts

schadenfreude: Pleasure derived from another person's misfortune

sinecure: A position requiring little or no work but providing a salary

sobriquet: A person's nickname or an assumed name

soliloquy: An act of speaking one's thoughts aloud when alone, especially in a play

stratagem: A plan or scheme especially one used to outwit an opponent

stultify: To cause someone to appear foolish or absurd; to render useless or ineffective

svelte: Slender and elegant in form or figure
The svelte design of the Vande Bharat Express marked a new aesthetic in Indian railway manufacturing.
bonhomie: The bonhomie between the two leaders at the summit masked deep disagreements on trade policy.
brinkmanship: The art of pursuing a dangerous policy to the limits of safety before stopping

brusque: Abrupt or offhand in speech or manner; rudely blunt

iconography: The visual images and symbols used in a work of art or the study of such imagery

idyll: An extremely happy or peaceful period or situation; a short pastoral poem

impregnable: Unable to be captured or broken into; strong enough to resist attack

incisive: Intelligently analytical and clear-thinking; sharp and direct

ineluctable: Unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable

ingratiate: To bring oneself into favour with someone by flattering or trying to please them

insipid: Lacking flavour, vigour, or interest; dull and uninspiring

interloper: A person who becomes involved in something without being invited; an intruder

abysmal: Extremely bad or severe; immeasurably deep

apostate: A person who renounces a religious or political belief or principle

aver: To state or assert positively and formally as a fact

coda: The concluding passage of a piece of music or literary work; a concluding event

oracle: A person or thing regarded as an infallible authority on something; a divine prophecy

progenitor: An ancestor or parent; a person who originates a movement or institution
Raja Ram Mohan Roy is regarded as the progenitor of the Indian renaissance and social reform movement.
dilatory: Slow to act; intended to cause delay

appurtenance: An accessory or other item associated with a particular activity or style of living

assiduity: Constant and close attention to what one is doing; diligence and perseverance

aphorism: A pithy observation containing a general truth; a concise statement of principle
Thiruvalluvar's aphorisms in the Tirukkural continue to guide Tamil ethical thought after two millennia.
antithetical: Directly opposed or contrasted; being the exact opposite

concomitant: Naturally accompanying or associated with something

contumacious: Stubbornly or wilfully disobedient to authority

prostrate: Lying stretched out on the ground face downward; completely overcome or helpless

penurious: Extremely poor; unwilling to spend money; stingy

pillory: To expose someone to public ridicule or abuse; a wooden frame for public punishment

propitiate: To win or regain the favour of someone by doing something to please them

oppugn: To call into question; to resist or oppose with argument

filigree: Ornamental work of fine wire; intricate and delicate ornamentation

furtive: Attempting to avoid notice or attention in a secretive manner; stealthy

frivolous: Not having any serious purpose or value; carefree and not serious

foist: To impose an unwelcome or unnecessary thing on someone without their consent

glean: To extract information from various sources; to gather grain left by reapers

gentry: People of good social position below the nobility; the upper middle class

genteel: Polite, refined, and respectable in an upper-class way, often affectedly so
The genteel facade of colonial Calcutta's clubs masked the brutal exploitation of the plantation economy.
grotto: A small picturesque cave especially an artificial one in a park or garden
The Ajanta grottos contain some of the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian mural painting.
hermitage: A secluded dwelling of a hermit; a retreat from worldly life
The ancient hermitages in the Himalayan foothills continue to attract seekers of spiritual solitude.
incumbent: Necessary for someone as a duty or responsibility; the current holder of an office

inconsequential: Not important or significant; trivial and irrelevant

kibitz: To look on and offer unwanted advice especially at a card game; to chat informally

kinship: Blood relationship; a sharing of characteristics or origins between things

liaise: To establish a working connection between people or organisations; to communicate and cooperate

luminary: A person who inspires or influences others in a particular field; a natural light-giving body

magnate: A wealthy or influential person especially in business or industry

malinger: To pretend to be ill in order to escape duty or work

maven: An expert or connoisseur in a particular field

miscreant: A person who behaves badly or breaks the law; a wrongdoer

militate: To be a powerful or conclusive factor in preventing something

maelstrom: A powerful whirlpool; a situation of confused and violent turmoil

notary: A person authorised to perform certain legal formalities especially to certify documents
