nihilist: A person who believes that life is meaningless and rejects all moral and religious principles
The existential despair of post-war literature reflected a nihilist worldview that questioned all established values.
overture: An orchestral introduction; an initial approach or proposal made to someone
India's diplomatic overtures to ASEAN nations strengthened the Act East policy framework.
ossify: To turn into bone; to become rigid and fixed in attitude or position
Bureaucratic processes ossify over decades until they serve the system rather than the citizen.
palisade: A fence of wooden stakes forming an enclosure for defence; a protective barrier
The ancient settlements along the Gangetic plain used wooden palisades as their primary defence.
parley: A conference between opposing sides to discuss terms; a negotiation
The ceasefire parley between the government and insurgent groups opened a window for lasting peace.
pedagogy: The method and practice of teaching especially as an academic subject
NEP 2020 advocates a shift from rote-learning pedagogy to experiential and inquiry-based approaches.
perdition: A state of eternal punishment and damnation; complete and utter ruin
The editorial warned that unchecked environmental destruction leads the nation toward ecological perdition.
peregrinate: To travel or wander from place to place especially on foot
Adi Shankaracharya peregrinated across India establishing four mathas at the cardinal points.
philistine: A person hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts; an uncultured person
The philistine decision to demolish heritage structures for parking lots drew condemnation from architects.
pliant: Easily bent or flexible; yielding readily to influence or persuasion
A pliant judiciary that bends to executive pressure undermines the separation of powers.
palaver: Prolonged and idle discussion; unnecessary fuss
The bureaucratic palaver around a simple building permit takes months of needless back-and-forth.
panache: Flamboyant confidence of style or manner; a grand or dashing manner
The young diplomat handled the crisis with remarkable panache that earned international admiration.
requite: To make appropriate return for a favour, service, or wrongdoing; to repay
The state must requite the sacrifices of armed forces personnel with adequate welfare measures.
repartee: Conversation or speech characterised by quick witty comments or replies
The legendary repartee between Nehru and Churchill during diplomatic encounters is well documented.
riposte: A quick clever reply to an insult or criticism; a quick return thrust in fencing
The finance minister's sharp riposte to opposition criticism silenced the hecklers in Parliament.
repertoire: A stock of skills, techniques, or items that a person habitually uses
India's diplomatic repertoire now includes climate leadership alongside traditional non-alignment principles.
rogue: A dishonest or unprincipled person; an entity that behaves in an aberrant way
Rogue elements within the bureaucracy undermined the implementation of the transparency initiative.
raillery: Good-humoured teasing; lighthearted mockery or banter
The friendly raillery between coalition partners masked genuine policy disagreements beneath.
sophist: A person who uses clever but false arguments; one who reasons with deceptive skill
The policy sophist defended the regressive tax by framing it as progressive reform.
schism: A split or division between strongly opposed parties caused by differences in opinion
The schism within the ruling party over economic policy threatened to bring down the coalition government.
surfeit: An excessive amount of something; overindulgence in eating or drinking
India faces a surfeit of regulations but a deficit of enforcement in environmental protection.
truism: A statement that is obviously true and says nothing new or interesting
That education empowers citizens is a truism that needs translation into actual budget priorities.
totalitarian: Relating to a system of government that is centralised and dictatorial requiring complete subservience
The Emergency period of 1975-77 exhibited totalitarian tendencies that tested India's democratic resilience.
travail: Painful or laborious effort; suffering or hardship
The travails of migrant workers during the pandemic lockdown exposed the fragility of India's informal economy.
tryst: A private romantic rendezvous; a meeting at an agreed time and place
Nehru's famous speech invoked India's tryst with destiny at the midnight hour of independence.
veritable: Used as an intensifier to qualify a metaphor; genuine and real
The annual Kumbh Mela is a veritable ocean of humanity with millions converging in a single city.
vociferate: To shout or cry out loudly and vehemently
Protesters vociferated their demands outside Parliament until the Speaker agreed to a discussion.
ablaze: Burning fiercely; filled with strong emotion or excitement
The freedom movement set the nation ablaze with patriotic fervour from coast to coast.
adjure: To urge or request someone solemnly or earnestly to do something
The judge adjured the witness to speak the truth without fear or favour.
amass: To gather together or accumulate a large amount of something over time
The industrialist amassed a fortune during the post-liberalisation boom of the 1990s.
arcadia: An idyllic region of rural contentment and simplicity; a pastoral paradise
The tourism brochure painted Coorg as an Arcadia of coffee plantations and misty hills.
behest: A person's orders or command; an authoritative request
The transfer was carried out at the behest of the chief minister despite service rules.
bequest: A legacy; something left to someone in a will or passed down as inheritance
Jamsetji Tata's bequest to the nation included institutions that became pillars of Indian science and industry.
blitz: An intensive and sudden military attack; a sudden concentrated effort
The government launched a vaccination blitz in rural districts to achieve herd immunity targets.
bravura: Great skill and brilliance shown in a performance; a bold display of daring
The surgeon's bravura performance during the complex separation of conjoined twins earned global recognition.
cadence: The rise and fall in pitch of the voice; a rhythmic flow of sounds or words
The cadence of classical Urdu poetry requires years of training to master.
cavalcade: A formal procession of people walking, riding, or driving vehicles
A cavalcade of diplomatic vehicles signalled the arrival of heads of state for the G20 summit.
cesspool: A storage pit for liquid waste; a disgusting or corrupt place
The report described the unregulated industrial zone as a cesspool of toxic waste and exploitation.
cipher: A secret code; a person or thing of no importance; the number zero
Without the cipher key, the encrypted diplomatic communications remained unreadable to interceptors.
citadel: A fortress typically on high ground protecting or dominating a city
The citadel of Golconda stood as the last bastion of Qutb Shahi resistance against Mughal expansion.
clout: Influence or power especially in politics or business; a heavy blow
Regional parties wield disproportionate clout in coalition governments at the Centre.
codify: To arrange laws or rules into a systematic code or collection
The Law Commission recommended codifying personal laws to ensure uniform civil standards.
cordon: A line of people or barriers preventing access to an area; to guard with a cordon
Security forces threw a cordon around the blast site within minutes of the explosion.
counterfeit: Made in imitation of something valuable with intent to deceive; forged
The RBI periodically issues advisories on identifying counterfeit currency notes in circulation.
dapper: Neat and trim in dress and appearance; stylishly elegant
The dapper diplomat's impeccable attire complemented his polished negotiation skills.
decamp: To depart suddenly or secretly especially to escape from somewhere
The accused decamped with crores of investor funds before the fraud was detected.
defalcation: Embezzlement of funds by a person trusted with them; misappropriation
The bank's defalcation scandal revealed systemic failures in internal audit mechanisms.
derogate: To detract from; to deviate from a standard or expectation
Any law that derogates from fundamental rights is subject to judicial review under Article 13.
disavow: To deny any responsibility for or connection with something
The government disavowed any involvement in the covert operation once it became public.
edifice: A large imposing building; a complex system of beliefs or institutions
The entire edifice of Indian democracy rests on the twin pillars of the Constitution and an independent judiciary.
emolument: A salary, fee, or profit from employment or office
Article 112 lists the emoluments of the President and other constitutional officers as charged expenditure.
enclave: A portion of territory surrounded by a larger territory whose inhabitants have different characteristics
Pondicherry remained a French enclave within independent India until its integration in 1954.
ensconce: To settle or establish comfortably and securely in a position
Once ensconced in power, the dynasty showed little inclination toward internal party democracy.
entreat: To ask someone earnestly or anxiously to do something; to plead
The villagers entreated the district magistrate for immediate relief after the devastating floods.
epitome: A person or thing that is a perfect example of a particular quality or type
Dr. APJ Abdul Kalam remains the epitome of humility combined with scientific achievement.
eschew: To deliberately avoid using or doing something; to abstain from
The Gandhian philosophy eschews violence as a means of achieving political change.
exhort: To strongly encourage or urge someone to do something
The Prime Minister exhorted citizens to adopt indigenous products under the vocal for local campaign.
gazette: An official journal or newspaper especially one published by the government
All government notifications must be published in the official gazette to acquire legal force.
gist: The substance or essential part of a speech or text; the main point
The gist of the Supreme Court ruling was that privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21.
hobnob: To mix socially especially with those of higher social status
The industrialist was known to hobnob with politicians across party lines at exclusive gatherings.
infirmity: Physical or mental weakness; a particular disease or ailment
Old age infirmity should not bar senior citizens from accessing pension benefits at their doorstep.
inveigle: To persuade someone through deception or flattery to do something
The fraudster inveigled elderly depositors into investing their savings in a fictitious scheme.
litigant: A person involved in a lawsuit or legal proceedings
The court directed that no litigant shall be denied justice merely due to inability to pay court fees.
mercenary: A professional soldier hired to serve in a foreign army; motivated primarily by money
Critics accused the private military contractors of being mercenary forces answerable to no democratic authority.
mete: To distribute or allot justice, punishment, or harsh treatment
The tribunal meted out strict penalties to companies violating pollution control norms.
mores: The essential or characteristic customs and conventions of a community
The changing mores of urban India present both opportunities and challenges for advertisers.
parity: The state or condition of being equal especially in status or pay
Achieving gender parity in parliamentary representation remains an unfulfilled constitutional aspiration.
perpetuate: To make something continue indefinitely; to preserve from extinction
Caste-based reservations were intended to correct historical injustice, not perpetuate identity politics.
pittance: A very small or inadequate amount of money or remuneration
Daily wage labourers work gruelling hours for a pittance that barely covers one meal a day.
potentate: A monarch or ruler especially an autocratic one
The erstwhile potentates of princely India traded sovereignty for privy purses during integration.
proviso: A condition or qualification attached to an agreement or statement
The trade agreement came with the proviso that both nations would review terms after five years.
purview: The scope of the influence or concerns of something; range of authority
Consumer protection now falls under the purview of a dedicated ministry with expanded powers.
retinue: A group of advisers or attendants accompanying an important person
The minister's large retinue of advisers and security personnel disrupted the rural health camp.
reverie: A state of being pleasantly lost in one's thoughts; a daydream
The poet's reverie about a united subcontinent found expression in verses that transcended political borders.
scion: A young shoot or twig; a descendant of a notable family
The scion of the industrial dynasty diversified the family business into renewable energy.
sentinel: A soldier or guard whose job is to stand and keep watch
The Supreme Court stands as a sentinel of fundamental rights against legislative overreach.
serf: An agricultural labourer bound to work on a particular estate; a feudal worker
The zamindari system reduced millions of Indian cultivators to the status of serfs on their own land.
sojourn: A temporary stay at a place; to stay somewhere temporarily
Gandhi's sojourn in South Africa transformed him from a barrister into a mass leader.
subsume: To include or absorb something into a larger category or group
The GST subsumed over a dozen central and state taxes into a single unified framework.
tithe: One tenth of annual produce paid as a tax; any small tax or levy
Medieval Indian rulers collected tithes from peasants that funded both warfare and public works.
tribunal: A court of justice or a body appointed to adjudicate on disputes
The National Green Tribunal has emerged as a critical forum for environmental justice in India.
usher: To show or guide someone somewhere; to cause or mark the start of something new
The 1991 reforms ushered in an era of economic liberalisation that transformed India's growth story.
vagrant: A person without a settled home or regular work who wanders from place to place
The Bombay Prevention of Begging Act criminalised vagrancy, drawing criticism from human rights advocates.
vanguard: A group of people leading the way in new developments; the foremost part of an advancing army
India has positioned itself at the vanguard of the global digital payments revolution through UPI.
vicar: A representative or deputy; a member of clergy acting as a substitute
The governor acts as the vicar of the President in state affairs under the constitutional framework.
whittle: To carve wood by cutting small pieces; to gradually reduce in size or amount
Successive amendments have whittled down the original scope of the Right to Information Act.
remit: The task or area of activity officially assigned to a person or organisation; to send payment
Monetary policy falls within the remit of the Reserve Bank of India, not the Finance Ministry.
exorcise: To drive out an evil spirit; to completely eliminate something unwanted
The nation must exorcise the demons of communalism if its secular fabric is to survive.
basalt: A dark fine-grained volcanic rock; the foundation layer of the earth's crust
The Deccan basalt formations created the fertile black soil that sustains Maharashtra's cotton cultivation.
billet: A place for a soldier to stay temporarily; to assign accommodation to military personnel
The army billeted relief troops in school buildings during the earthquake response operations.
buccaneer: A pirate; a daring and unscrupulous adventurer in business or politics
The corporate buccaneers of the early liberalisation era bent every rule to build business empires.
careen: To tilt or sway dangerously to one side while moving; to rush headlong
The economy careened from one crisis to another during the balance of payments emergency of 1991.
cognate: Related by birth; having the same linguistic root or origin
Hindi and Marathi are cognate languages sharing Sanskrit roots and grammatical structures.
corsair: European corsairs terrorised the Malabar coast until the Maratha navy curbed their activities.
crypt: An underground room or vault beneath a church used as a burial place
The crypts beneath Goa's ancient churches contain the remains of Portuguese-era missionaries.
dudgeon: A feeling of deep offence or resentment
The ambassador left in high dudgeon after the host nation downgraded the diplomatic reception.
echelon: A level or rank in an organisation or society; a formation of troops in parallel rows
Policy decisions at the highest echelons of government rarely reflect ground-level implementation challenges.
effete: Affected, over-refined, and ineffectual; no longer capable of effective action
The effete aristocracy of the late Mughal period could not prevent the rise of regional powers.
elixir: A magical preparation supposedly able to cure all diseases; a preparation that is invigorating
There is no elixir for economic growth other than sustained investment in education and infrastructure.
fiefdom: An estate of land held under the feudal system; an area of operation controlled by a person
Each department head treated their ministry as a personal fiefdom resistant to any coordination.
flotsam: Wreckage of a ship found floating; useless or discarded objects
The flotsam of abandoned industrial projects litters the landscape of India's special economic zones.
foray: A sudden attack or incursion into enemy territory; an initial attempt at a new activity
India's first foray into nuclear energy began with the establishment of the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre.
ghetto: A part of a city occupied by a minority group; an isolated or segregated area
Urban planning must break the pattern of religious ghettos that deepen communal segregation.
gossamer: A fine filmy substance of cobwebs; something very light, thin, and insubstantial
The gossamer promises of the election manifesto evaporated within months of the government taking office.
imprimatur: An official licence to print or publish; a person's acceptance or approval
No policy directive carries weight without the imprimatur of the Prime Minister's Office.
lacuna: An unfilled space or gap; a missing portion in a text or argument
The lacuna in India's data protection framework was addressed by the new Digital Personal Data Protection Act.
levee: An embankment built to prevent river flooding; a formal reception of visitors
The breach of the levee along the Kosi river displaced millions in Bihar during the 2008 floods.
martyr: A person who suffers or dies for a great cause or belief; one who seeks sympathy by exaggerating suffering
Bhagat Singh's legacy as a martyr for Indian independence continues to inspire revolutionary thought.
memoir: A historical account written from personal knowledge or special sources
The retired diplomat's memoir revealed backroom negotiations that shaped India's nuclear deal.
mountebank: A person who deceives others especially to cheat them out of money; a charlatan
The mountebank posing as a spiritual healer defrauded thousands of vulnerable devotees.
palimpsest: A manuscript on which earlier writing has been erased to make room for later writing; something bearing visible traces of an earlier form
India's cities are palimpsests where Mughal architecture, colonial bungalows, and glass towers coexist.
patrician: An aristocrat or nobleman; belonging to the aristocracy
The patrician class of colonial India collaborated with British administrators to maintain social hierarchy.
pecuniary: Relating to or consisting of money; financial
Public servants must declare any pecuniary interest that could conflict with their official duties.
plebiscite: A direct vote by the electorate on an important public question
The unresolved question of a plebiscite in Kashmir remains a contentious issue in India-Pakistan relations.
quay: A wharf or platform constructed alongside water for loading and unloading ships
The historic quays of Kochi bear witness to centuries of spice trade with Arab and European merchants.
rampart: A defensive wall of a castle or walled city with a broad top and usually a stone parapet
The ramparts of the Red Fort served as the backdrop for India's first Independence Day address.
relic: An object surviving from an earlier time; a part of a holy person's body kept as an object of reverence
The Ashoka Pillar at Sarnath is a relic of Mauryan artistry that became India's national emblem.
rudiment: The first principles of a subject; an undeveloped or basic form of something
Every civil servant must grasp the rudiments of public finance before assuming a district posting.
sepulchre: A small room or monument cut in rock or built of stone in which a dead person is buried
The ancient sepulchres discovered along the Deccan plateau revealed burial practices of megalithic cultures.
squalid: Extremely dirty and unpleasant especially as a result of poverty or neglect
The squalid conditions in urban slums demand urgent intervention in sanitation and housing.
stricture: A severe criticism or restriction; an abnormal narrowing of a passage in the body
The court passed strictures against the police department for custodial violence during investigations.
tincture: A slight trace of something; a medicine made by dissolving a substance in alcohol
Even a tincture of bias in judicial proceedings undermines the credibility of the entire justice system.
trope: A figurative use of a word; a common or overused theme or device in creative works
The trope of the honest bureaucrat fighting the system has become a staple of Indian cinema.
vagary: An unexpected and inexplicable change in behaviour or situation
The vagaries of the Indian monsoon make agricultural planning a perennial gamble for millions of farmers.
verve: Great energy, enthusiasm, and liveliness in expression or performance
The young parliamentarian's verve during debates brought fresh energy to the legislative proceedings.
vim: The startup ecosystem in Bengaluru operates with a vim that rivals Silicon Valley's best.
