apiary: A place where bees are kept
archaeologist: A person who studies ancient cultures
glutton: A person who eats too much
aviary: A place where birds are kept
illiterate: One who cannot read or write
autocracy: A government by a single ruler
polyglot: A person who speaks many languages
constellation: A group of stars forming a pattern
oligarchy: A government by a few powerful people
genocide: Killing of entire population or race
donor: One who donates money for a cause
fatalist: A person who believes in fate
optimist: One who looks at the bright side of things
pessimist: One who looks at the dark side of things
hypocrite: One who pretends to be what he is not
teetotaler: One who abstains from alcoholic drinks
neutral: One who is neither for nor against
mortuary: A place where dead bodies are kept
abattoir: A place where animals are slaughtered
barracks: A place where soldiers are housed
pharmacy: A place where medicines are prepared and sold
mint: A place where coins are made
winery: A place where wine is made
hangar: A place where aircrafts are kept
anthropology: Study of human societies and cultures
dermatology: Study of skin and its diseases
cardiology: Study of heart and its diseases
atheist: A person who does not believe in God
agnostic: A person who doubts the existence of God
recluse: One who lives alone and avoids society
gullible: A person who is easily deceived
omnipresent: A person present everywhere at the same time
granary: A building where grain is stored
arsenal: A place where weapons are stored
somnambulist: A person who walks in sleep
archaeology: A study of ancient civilizations through artifacts
geology: The study of rocks and minerals
bilingual: A person who can speak two languages
omnivore: A person who eats both plants and meat
herbivore: A person who eats only plants
carnivore: A person who eats only meat
anarchy: A state where no government exists
An (without) + archos (ruler). Complete absence of government or organized authority.
monarchy: A government by a single hereditary ruler
Mono (one) + archos (ruler). Rule by a king or queen, usually hereditary.
bureaucracy: A government run by officials following rigid rules and procedures
Bureau (desk/office) + kratos (rule). Administration by non-elected officials bound by procedure.
technocracy: A government by experts in science and technology
Techne (skill) + kratos (rule). Government run by technical experts rather than elected politicians.
meritocracy: A government based on merit and ability
Merit (deserving) + kratos (rule). System where advancement depends on talent and effort.
stratocracy: Stratos (army) + kratos (rule). State governed directly by the armed forces.
kleptocracy: A government where rulers exploit people for personal wealth
Kleptes (thief) + kratos (rule). Corrupt government where leaders steal public resources.
republic: A country with no king or queen where people elect their leaders
From Latin res publica (public affair). State where supreme power rests with citizens and their elected representatives.
bicameral: A system with two legislative chambers
Bi (two) + camera (chamber). Like India's Parliament with Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha.
unicameral: A system with a single legislative chamber
Uni (one) + camera (chamber). Legislature with only one house, like many Indian state assemblies.
matriarchy: A society governed by women or mothers
Mater (mother) + archos (ruler). Social system where women hold primary authority.
patriarchy: A society governed by men or fathers
Pater (father) + archos (ruler). Social system where men hold primary authority.
despot: A ruler who exercises absolute power cruelly
From Greek despotes (master). A tyrant who uses unlimited power oppressively.
nepotism: The practice of giving government jobs to relatives
From Latin nepos (nephew). Favouring family members for positions of power.
secularism: The principle of separating religion from government
From Latin saecularis (worldly). Core principle of the Indian Constitution — no state religion.
suffrage: The right of citizens to vote in elections
From Latin suffragium (vote). Universal adult suffrage in India under Article 326.
decree: An official order issued by a person in authority
From Latin decretum (decided). A formal order by a head of state or court of law.
treaty: A formal agreement between two or more countries
From Latin tractatus (handling). Formally concluded agreement between nations.
ambassador: The highest-ranking diplomatic representative sent to another country
From Latin ambactus (servant). Top diplomat representing one nation in another.
arbitrator: A person appointed to settle a dispute outside court
From Latin arbiter (judge). Neutral party chosen to resolve disputes, common in labour cases.
jury: A body of citizens sworn to deliver a verdict in court
From Latin jurare (to swear). Group hearing evidence and deciding guilt or innocence.
monopoly: Exclusive control of trade in a commodity by a single entity
Mono (one) + polein (to sell). Regulated by CCI in India to prevent unfair practices.
oligopoly: A market controlled by a small number of sellers
Oligo (few) + polein (to sell). Few dominant sellers, like Indian telecom or aviation.
monopsony: A market where there is only one buyer
Mono (one) + opsonia (purchase). Single buyer controls the market — opposite of monopoly.
traitor: A person who betrays their own country
From Latin traditor (one who hands over). One who commits treason against their nation.
gazette: An official government journal publishing laws and notifications
From Italian gazzetta. Official publication for laws, appointments, and government orders.
manifesto: A public declaration of policies issued by a political party before elections
From Latin manifestus (clear). Document stating a party's promises and agenda.
embargo: A temporary ban on trade with a particular country
From Spanish embargar (to restrain). Official prohibition on commerce with a nation.
tariff: A tax imposed on goods imported into a country
From Arabic ta'rifa (notification). Import duty collected by customs authority.
excise: A tax levied on goods manufactured within a country
From Latin excidere (to cut out). Indirect tax on domestic manufacture of goods.
subsidy: Financial aid given by the government to support an industry or keep prices low
From Latin subsidium (help). Government assistance like fertiliser or food subsidy in India.
imperialism: The policy of extending a country's power over other territories
From Latin imperium (command). Domination over foreign lands, as the British did in India.
feudalism: A medieval system where peasants worked land owned by lords
From Latin feudum (estate). Social hierarchy of lords and serfs in exchange for land and protection.
capitalism: An economic system based on private ownership and free markets
From Latin capitalis (of the head). Trade and industry controlled by private owners for profit.
communism: A system where the community owns all means of production
From Latin communis (shared). All property publicly owned; each works by ability, receives by need.
ceasefire: A formal agreement to stop fighting temporarily
Cease + fire. Temporary halt in hostilities, often preceding peace negotiations.
armistice: A formal agreement ending a war
From Latin arma (arms) + stitium (stoppage). Formal end to fighting, like the 1918 Armistice.
espionage: The practice of spying to obtain secret political or military information
From French espionner (to spy). Covert gathering of classified information by agents.
amnesty: A general pardon granted by the government to a group of offenders
From Greek amnestia (forgetfulness). Official forgiveness for political crimes, like tax amnesty schemes.
parole: Conditional early release of a prisoner before full sentence is served
From French parole (word of honour). Prisoner released on promise of good behaviour.
emigrant: A person who leaves their own country to settle in another
From Latin emigrare (to move away). One who departs from their home country.
immigrant: A person who comes to live permanently in a foreign country
From Latin immigrare (to move into). One who enters and settles in a new country.
refugee: A person forced to leave their country due to war or persecution
From Latin refugium (shelter). Protected under UN Refugee Convention of 1951.
expatriate: A person living outside their native country
From Latin ex (out) + patria (fatherland). One residing abroad, often for work.
infiltrator: A person who enters a country illegally
From Latin infiltrare (to strain in). One who crosses borders without authorisation.
charter: A written document granting special rights or privileges
From Latin charta (paper). Formal grant of rights, like the UN Charter or a university charter.
cartographer: Carta (map) + graphein (to write). One who makes maps, like those at Survey of India.
lexicographer: Lexicon (dictionary) + graphein (to write). One who writes and edits dictionaries.
calligrapher: A person who has beautiful handwriting
Kalli (beautiful) + graphein (to write). One skilled in decorative handwriting or lettering.
graphologist: A person who analyses handwriting to assess personality
Grapho (writing) + logos (study). One who studies handwriting to determine character traits.
etymologist: A person who traces the origin and history of words
Etymon (true sense) + logos (study). One who researches how words originated and evolved.
linguist: A person who is skilled in many languages
From Latin lingua (language). One who studies or is proficient in multiple languages.
misogynist: Miso (hatred) + gyne (woman). One who harbours deep prejudice against women.
misandrist: Miso (hatred) + aner/andros (man). One who harbours deep prejudice against men.
charlatan: A person who falsely claims to have special knowledge or skill
From Italian ciarlatano (babbler). A fraud or quack pretending expertise they do not possess.
sycophant: A person who flatters those in power for personal gain
From Greek sykophantes (informer). One who seeks favour through excessive flattery of the powerful.
martyr: A person who sacrifices their life for a cause or belief
From Greek martys (witness). One who suffers death for a principle, like India's freedom fighters.
novice: A person who is new to a profession or activity
From Latin novus (new). A beginner who lacks experience in a particular field.
veteran: A person with long experience in a particular field
From Latin vetus (old). One with extensive experience, especially in military or a profession.
nomad: A person who moves from place to place without a fixed home
From Greek nomas (wandering). One who travels constantly, like pastoral communities of Rajasthan.
hermit: A person who lives alone away from society
From Greek eremites (desert dweller). One who lives in solitude, often for spiritual reasons.
ascetic: A person who practises severe self-discipline and avoids all indulgence
From Greek askesis (exercise/training). One who renounces comforts, like many Indian saints and monks.
hedonist: A person who believes pleasure is the highest good in life
From Greek hedone (pleasure). One who pursues sensual pleasure as life's primary goal.
stoic: A person who endures pain and hardship without complaint
From Greek Stoa (porch where philosopher Zeno taught). One indifferent to pleasure or pain.
altruist: A person who works selflessly for the welfare of others
From Latin alter (other). One who puts others' wellbeing above their own interests.
egoist: A person who thinks only of their own interests
From Latin ego (I). One who is excessively self-centred in all matters.
narcissist: A person who is excessively in love with their own appearance
From Greek myth of Narcissus who fell in love with his own reflection in water.
amateur: A person who does something for pleasure and not as a profession
From Latin amator (lover). One who engages in an activity as a hobby, not for money.
ambidextrous: A person who can use both hands equally well
Ambi (both) + dexter (right hand). Equally skilled with left and right hands.
ophthalmologist: A person who treats diseases of the eyes
Ophthalmos (eye) + logos (study). Medical doctor specialising in eye diseases and surgery.
orthopedist: A person who treats diseases of bones and joints
Ortho (straight) + pais (child). Doctor specialising in the musculoskeletal system.
pediatrician: A doctor who specialises in treating children
Pais (child) + iatros (healer). Physician focused on medical care of infants and children.
geriatrician: A doctor who specialises in treating elderly people
Geras (old age) + iatros (healer). Physician focused on healthcare of the elderly.
psychologist: A person who studies the mind and human behaviour
Psyche (mind) + logos (study). One who researches mental processes and behaviour.
coroner: A person who examines dead bodies to determine the cause of death
From Latin corona (crown officer). Official who investigates suspicious or unexplained deaths.
taxidermist: A person who stuffs and mounts the skins of dead animals
Taxis (arrangement) + derma (skin). One who preserves animal bodies by mounting them.
veterinarian: A doctor who treats diseases of animals
From Latin veterinae (cattle). Medical professional treating animal diseases and injuries.
architect: A person who designs buildings and structures
From Greek archi (chief) + tekton (builder). One who plans and designs buildings.
playwright: A person who writes plays for the theatre
Play + wright (maker). One who composes dramatic works for stage performance.
lyricist: A person who writes the words for songs
From Greek lyrikos (of the lyre). One who writes lyrics or words for music.
cobbler: A person who makes and repairs shoes
From Middle English cobeler. A craftsperson who makes and mends footwear.
blacksmith: A person who works with iron and forges metal objects
Black (colour of iron oxide) + smith (worker). One who shapes iron by heating and hammering.
goldsmith: A person who makes jewellery from gold and silver
Gold + smith (worker). Craftsperson making ornaments from precious metals.
incorrigible: A person whose bad habits cannot be corrected
In (not) + corrigere (to correct). One whose behaviour is beyond reform.
invincible: A person who cannot be defeated
In (not) + vincere (to conquer). One who cannot be overcome or beaten.
cannibal: A person who eats human flesh
From Spanish Caníbal (Caribs people). One who consumes the flesh of their own species.
compere: A person who introduces performers at a stage show or event
From French compère (godfather). The host who introduces acts and entertains the audience.
anatomist: A person who studies the structure of the human body
From Greek anatome (dissection). One who studies the physical structure of organisms.
carpenter: A person who makes articles from wood
From Latin carpentarius (carriage maker). One who builds and repairs wooden structures.
mason: A person who builds with stone or brick
From Old French masson (stone worker). One skilled in constructing walls and buildings from stone or brick.
pilgrim: A person who travels to a holy place as an act of devotion
From Latin peregrinus (foreigner). One who journeys to a sacred site, like Kashi or Amarnath.
missionary: A person sent to a foreign country to spread religious teachings
From Latin missionem (sending). One dispatched abroad to promote a faith and do charitable work.
bakery: A place where bread and cakes are made and sold
From Dutch bakkerij. An establishment for baking and selling bread and confections.
brewery: A place where beer is brewed
From brew + ery. An industrial establishment for making beer.
distillery: A place where spirits like whisky are made by distillation
From Latin distillare (to drip). Factory producing alcoholic spirits through distillation.
tannery: A place where leather is made from animal hides
From Latin tannare (to tan). Factory where animal skins are processed into leather.
foundry: A place where metals are melted and cast into shapes
From Latin fundere (to pour). Workshop where metals are melted and poured into moulds.
nursery: A place where young plants and trees are grown for sale
From Latin nutrire (to nourish). Establishment growing seedlings for transplanting.
orchard: A place where fruit trees are cultivated
From Old English ortgeard (garden). Land planted with fruit-bearing trees like mango or apple.
vineyard: A place where grapevines are cultivated for winemaking
From Latin vinea (vine). Land where grapes are grown, like those in Nashik, Maharashtra.
hospital: A building where patients receive medical treatment and stay overnight
From Latin hospes (guest). Institution providing comprehensive medical care with beds.
sanatorium: A place where the sick go for long-term recovery, especially from tuberculosis
From Latin sanare (to heal). Residential facility for prolonged convalescence.
orphanage: A place where orphans are housed and cared for
From Greek orphanos (parentless). Institution providing shelter and care for parentless children.
old age home: A place where old people are cared for
Residential facility providing care and shelter for elderly citizens who need assistance.
museum: A building where historical objects and artworks are displayed
From Greek mouseion (seat of the Muses). Institution preserving and exhibiting artefacts of cultural value.
archive: A place where government records and historical documents are preserved
From Greek arkheion (public records). Repository of official documents, like the National Archives of India.
library: A room or building for housing a collection of books for reading
From Latin liber (book). Institution maintaining a collection of books for public or private use.
mycology: Mykes (fungus) + logos (study). Branch of biology concerned with the study of fungi.
neurology: The study of the nervous system and its disorders
Neuron (nerve) + logos (study). Medical speciality dealing with disorders of the brain and nerves.
oncology: The study of tumours and cancer
Onkos (mass/tumour) + logos (study). Branch of medicine focused on diagnosis and treatment of cancer.
pathology: The study of diseases and their causes
Pathos (suffering) + logos (study). Examination of body tissues and fluids to diagnose disease.
nephrology: The study of the kidneys and their diseases
Nephros (kidney) + logos (study). Medical speciality dealing with kidney function and disease.
endocrinology: The study of hormones and the endocrine system
Endon (within) + krinein (to separate) + logos. Study of hormone-producing glands like thyroid and pituitary.
hematology: The study of blood and blood-forming organs
Haima (blood) + logos (study). Branch of medicine dealing with blood disorders like anaemia.
hepatology: The study of the liver and its diseases
Hepar (liver) + logos (study). Branch of medicine focused on liver, gallbladder, and biliary system.
gastroenterology: The study of the stomach and intestines
Gaster (stomach) + enteron (intestine) + logos. Study of the entire digestive system.
paleontology: The study of fossils and ancient life forms
Palaios (ancient) + ontos (being) + logos. Study of prehistoric life through fossil evidence.
numismatics: The study of coins and medals
From Greek nomisma (coin). Study of coins, paper money, and medals — also a popular collecting hobby.
philately: The study of stamps and postal history
Philos (loving) + ateleia (exemption from tax, i.e. postage). Collecting and studying postage stamps.
epigraphy: The study of ancient inscriptions carved on stone or metal
Epi (upon) + graphein (to write). Study of inscriptions, vital for Indian historical research like Ashoka's edicts.
paleography: The study of old handwriting and historical manuscripts
Palaios (ancient) + graphein (to write). Deciphering historical scripts and documents.
cosmology: The study of the origin and evolution of the universe
Kosmos (universe) + logos (study). Branch of physics studying the origin, structure, and fate of the universe.
meteorology: The study of weather and atmospheric conditions
Meteora (atmospheric phenomena) + logos. Science of weather, as practised by IMD in India.
climatology: The study of climate patterns over long periods
Klima (inclination/zone) + logos. Study of long-term weather patterns, distinct from day-to-day meteorology.
ecology: The study of the relationship between living organisms and their environment
Oikos (house/habitat) + logos. Study of how organisms interact with their environment.
genetics: The study of heredity and genetic variation in organisms
From Greek genesis (origin). Science of genes, heredity, and variation in living organisms.
cytology: The study of cells and their structure
Kytos (cell) + logos (study). Branch of biology examining cell structure and function under microscope.
histology: Histos (tissue) + logos (study). Microscopic examination of biological tissues.
taxonomy: The science of classification of organisms into groups
Taxis (arrangement) + nomia (method). System of naming and classifying living organisms.
physiography: The study of the physical features of the earth's surface
Physis (nature) + graphein (to write). Description of natural landforms and physical processes of Earth.
physiology: The study of the structure and function of the human body
Physis (nature) + logos (study). How organs and systems of the body perform their functions.
toxicology: The study of poisons and their effects
Toxikon (poison) + logos. Science of poisons, their detection, and treatment of poisoning.
pharmacology: The study of drugs and their effects on the body
Pharmakon (drug) + logos. Science of how medicines work, their uses, and side effects.
embryology: The study of the formation and development of embryos
Embryon (unborn child) + logos. Study of how organisms develop from fertilisation to birth.
cartography: The science of making maps and charts
Carta (map) + graphein (to write). Art and science of map-making, vital for Survey of India.
gerontology: The study of the ageing process and problems of elderly people
Geron (old person) + logos. Scientific study of ageing — broader than geriatrics which is medical treatment.
philology: The study of languages and their structure
Philos (loving) + logos (word). Historical and comparative study of language through texts.
phonetics: The study of the production and perception of speech sounds
Phone (sound/voice) + tics. Science of speech sounds — how they are made, transmitted, and heard.
nyctophobia: Nyx (night) + phobos (fear). Intense irrational fear of the dark or nighttime.
pyrophobia: Pyr (fire) + phobos (fear). Intense dread of fire or flames.
cynophobia: Kyon (dog) + phobos (fear). One of the most common animal phobias worldwide.
ailurophobia: Ailouros (cat) + phobos (fear). Intense dread of cats, also called gatophobia.
ophidiophobia: Ophis (snake) + phobos (fear). Fear of snakes, very common in tropical regions like India.
autophobia: An extreme fear of being alone
Auto (self) + phobos (fear). Intense anxiety about being isolated or alone.
thanatophobia: An extreme fear of death or dying
Thanatos (death) + phobos (fear). Overwhelming dread of one's own death or the process of dying.
ochlophobia: An extreme fear of crowds or public places
Ochlos (crowd) + phobos (fear). Fear of being in large gatherings of people.
aviophobia: An extreme fear of flying in an aircraft
Avis (bird/flight) + phobos (fear). Intense anxiety about travelling by aeroplane.
astraphobia: An extreme fear of thunder and lightning
Astrape (lightning) + phobos (fear). Intense dread triggered by thunderstorms.
taphophobia: An extreme fear of being buried alive
Taphos (grave) + phobos (fear). Dread of being entombed alive, common in Victorian era.
hemophobia: Haima (blood) + phobos (fear). Intense anxiety at the sight of blood.
kleptomania: An obsessive desire to steal things
Kleptes (thief) + mania (madness). Compulsive urge to steal, regardless of need or value.
pyromania: An obsessive desire to set things on fire
Pyr (fire) + mania (madness). Compulsive urge to deliberately set fires.
megalomania: An obsession with one's own importance and power
Megalo (great) + mania (madness). Delusion of grandeur and obsession with power.
dipsomania: An uncontrollable craving for alcoholic drink
Dipsa (thirst) + mania (madness). Compulsive and uncontrollable desire for alcohol.
egomania: Ego (I/self) + mania (madness). Pathological preoccupation with oneself to the exclusion of others.
bibliomania: An obsessive craving to be near books
Biblio (book) + mania (madness). Obsessive collecting of books far beyond any practical use.
graphomania: Grapho (writing) + mania (madness). Compulsive desire to write constantly.
mariticide: The killing of one's own spouse
Maritus (husband) + caedere (to kill). Murder of one's husband. Killing of wife is uxoricide.
uxoricide: The killing of one's own wife
Uxor (wife) + caedere (to kill). The specific term for murder of one's wife.
homicide: The killing of a human being
Homo (human) + caedere (to kill). The general term for one person killing another.
suicide: Sui (of oneself) + caedere (to kill). Act of deliberately ending one's own life.
ethnic cleansing: The killing of a large number of people from a particular ethnic group
Systematic elimination of an ethnic or religious group from a region through violence or deportation.
germicide: The destruction of germs and bacteria
Germ + caedere (to kill). Substance that destroys harmful microorganisms.
insecticide: A chemical substance used to kill insects
Insect + caedere (to kill). Agent used to eliminate harmful insects in agriculture.
herbicide: A chemical substance used to kill weeds
Herba (plant) + caedere (to kill). Chemical agent that destroys unwanted plants or weeds.
fungicide: A chemical substance used to kill fungi
Fungus + caedere (to kill). Agent used to destroy fungal infections in crops.
anthology: A collection of poems or literary pieces
Anthos (flower) + logia (collection). A curated collection of poems, stories, or essays by various authors.
bibliography: A list of books and writings on a particular subject
Biblio (book) + graphein (to write). Systematic list of sources and references on a topic.
glossary: A list of words with their meanings, especially for a specialised field
From Greek glossa (tongue/language). An alphabetical list of terms with definitions for a specific domain.
armada: A fleet of ships sailing together
From Spanish armada (armed force). A large group of warships, like the Spanish Armada of 1588.
convoy: A group of ships travelling together for protection
From French convoyer (to escort). Ships or vehicles travelling together under armed protection.
pack: From Middle English pak. The collective noun for wolves hunting and living together.
shoal: A group of fish swimming together
From Old English scolu (troop). A large number of fish moving together in water.
swarm: A large group of flying insects
From Old English swearm. A dense cluster of insects, especially bees, moving together.
herd: A group of cattle or elephants
From Old English heord. Large group of hoofed animals grazing or moving together.
flock: A group of sheep or birds
From Old English flocc. Collective noun for sheep, goats, or birds gathered together.
congregation: A group of people gathered for a religious service
From Latin congregare (to flock together). People gathered for worship in a church or temple.
troupe: A group of actors or performers
From French troupe (company). A company of theatrical performers or dancers.
choir: A group of singers performing together
From Greek khoros (dance/singing). An organized group of singers, especially in a church or concert.
orchestra: A group of musicians playing instruments together
From Greek orkhestra (dancing place). Large ensemble of instrumentalists performing classical music.
squadron: A group of warships under one commander
From Italian squadrone (soldiers drawn up in a square). Naval or air force unit under single command.
archipelago: A group of islands clustered together
From Greek archi (chief) + pelagos (sea). A chain of islands, like the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.
cabal: A group of people working together on a secret plan
From Hebrew qabbalah (tradition). A small secretive political group plotting together.
cartel: A group of business firms acting together to fix prices
From German Kartell (agreement). Alliance of companies controlling prices, investigated by CCI in India.
invisible: Something that cannot be seen with the naked eye
In (not) + visibilis (able to be seen). Not perceptible by the eye.
inaudible: In (not) + audire (to hear). Too quiet or faint to be heard.
illegible: Something that cannot be read or deciphered
In (not) + legere (to read). Handwriting or print too unclear to be read.
intangible: Something that cannot be touched or grasped physically
In (not) + tangere (to touch). Abstract things like goodwill or reputation that have no physical form.
indelible: Something that cannot be erased or removed
In (not) + delere (to destroy). Permanent and impossible to remove, like indelible ink used in Indian elections.
inimitable: Something that cannot be imitated or matched
In (not) + imitare (to copy). So unique or exceptional that it cannot be copied.
inevitable: Something that is bound to happen and cannot be avoided
In (not) + evitare (to avoid). Certain to occur, impossible to prevent.
irreversible: Something that cannot be reversed or undone
Ir (not) + reversus (turned back). A change or process that cannot be returned to its original state.
irreparable: Something that cannot be repaired or made good
Ir (not) + reparare (to repair). Damage so severe it cannot be fixed.
irrevocable: Something that cannot be recalled or taken back once done
Ir (not) + revocare (to call back). A decision or action that cannot be changed or withdrawn.
inexplicable: Something that cannot be understood or explained
In (not) + explicare (to unfold). Too strange or complex to be accounted for.
ephemeral: Something that lasts for a very short time
From Greek ephemeros (lasting only a day). Short-lived and fleeting, like morning dew.
eternal: From Latin aeternus (everlasting). Without beginning or end, lasting for all time.
annual: From Latin annus (year). Occurring once every year, like annual budget sessions in Parliament.
biennial: Something that happens once every two years
Bi (two) + annus (year). Occurring every two years. Not to be confused with biannual (twice a year).
biannual: Something that happens twice a year
Bi (two) + annus (year). Occurring two times per year, unlike biennial which is once in two years.
triennial: Something that happens once every three years
Tri (three) + annus (year). Occurring every three years.
perennial: Something that continues throughout the year or for many years
Per (through) + annus (year). Lasting indefinitely, like perennial rivers such as the Ganga.
egotism: An act of speaking about oneself too much
From Latin ego (I). Excessive talking about oneself and one's own achievements.
polygyny: The practice of having more than one wife at a time
Poly (many) + gyne (woman). Marriage to multiple wives simultaneously.
polyandry: The practice of having more than one husband at a time
Poly (many) + aner/andros (man). Marriage to multiple husbands, practised in parts of Kinnaur, Himachal Pradesh.
monogamy: The practice of having only one spouse at a time
Mono (one) + gamos (marriage). Marriage to a single partner, legally mandated in India.
bigamy: The crime of marrying someone while already legally married to another
Bi (two) + gamos (marriage). Illegal in India under Section 494 of IPC.
polygamy: The practice of having many spouses
Poly (many) + gamos (marriage). General term for multiple marriages, includes polygyny and polyandry.
epitaph: Words written on a tombstone in memory of the dead
Epi (upon) + taphos (tomb). Inscription honouring the deceased, carved on a gravestone.
epigram: A short witty saying expressing a general truth
Epi (upon) + gramma (writing). A brief, clever, and memorable statement.
obituary: A notice of a person's death published in a newspaper
From Latin obitus (death). Published account of a deceased person's life and achievements.
eulogy: A speech or piece of writing praising someone who has recently died
Eu (well) + logos (word). A tribute delivered at a funeral honouring the deceased.
elegy: A poem of serious reflection, especially mourning the dead
From Greek elegeia (mournful poem). A sorrowful poem lamenting a person's death.
epilogue: The concluding section of a book or play
Epi (after) + logos (word). Final section that comments on or concludes the main narrative.
prologue: The introductory section of a book or play
Pro (before) + logos (word). Opening section that introduces the story or sets the scene.
epithet: A word or phrase used to describe someone's character
From Greek epitheton (added). A descriptive term like 'Mahatma' for Gandhi or 'Iron Man' for Sardar Patel.
euphemism: A mild or indirect expression used instead of a harsh or blunt one
Eu (well) + pheme (speaking). Saying 'passed away' instead of 'died' to soften the impact.
synecdoche: Using a part to represent the whole or the whole to represent a part
From Greek synekdoche (taking together). Saying 'all hands on deck' where hands represent sailors.
paradox: A statement that contradicts itself but may contain a hidden truth
Para (contrary) + doxa (opinion). Seemingly absurd statement that reveals deeper meaning.
oxymoron: A figure of speech combining two contradictory terms
Oxys (sharp) + moros (dull). Combination like 'bitter sweet' or 'living dead'.
idiom: An expression whose meaning cannot be understood from the individual words
From Greek idios (one's own). A phrase like 'kick the bucket' meaning to die.
proverb: A short well-known saying stating a general truth or piece of advice
From Latin proverbium (common saying). Traditional wisdom like 'A stitch in time saves nine'.
tirade: A long and aggressive speech by one person
From Italian tirata (volley). A lengthy, angry, and critical speech denouncing something.
soliloquy: A speech in a play where a character speaks their thoughts aloud while alone
Solus (alone) + loqui (to speak). Character revealing inner thoughts to the audience, as in Shakespeare.
monologue: A long speech by one person in a conversation or performance
Mono (one) + logos (word). Extended speech by a single speaker, unlike a soliloquy not necessarily alone.
dialogue: A conversation between two or more people
Dia (through/between) + logos (word). Exchange of ideas between two or more speakers.
oratory: The art of effective or persuasive public speaking
From Latin orare (to speak). The skill of powerful public speaking, as practised by leaders like Nehru.
elocution: The art of clear and expressive speech with correct pronunciation
From Latin eloqui (to speak out). Training in distinct pronunciation and effective delivery.
rhetoric: The use of language to persuade or influence, sometimes insincerely
From Greek rhetor (speaker). Art of persuasion through language, often implying empty words.
eloquence: The power of speaking fluently and expressively
From Latin eloquentia (speaking out). Gift of expressive and moving speech.
autobiography: The life story of a person written by that person
Auto (self) + bio (life) + graphein (to write). Self-written life account, like 'The Story of My Experiments with Truth' by Gandhi.
biography: The life story of a person written by someone else
Bio (life) + graphein (to write). Account of a person's life written by another author.
chronicle: A book or account recording events in the order they happened
From Greek khronos (time). Historical record of events in chronological order.
diary: A personal daily record of events and thoughts
From Latin diarium (daily allowance). Day-by-day private account of one's activities and reflections.
encyclopedia: A book giving information on all subjects or one subject arranged alphabetically
Enkyklios (circular/general) + paideia (education). Comprehensive reference work covering all branches of knowledge.
atlas: A book of maps and geographical charts
Named after Atlas, the Greek Titan who held up the sky. Collection of maps published as a bound volume.
almanac: A yearly calendar with weather forecasts, astronomical data, and other information
From Arabic al-manakh (climate). Annual publication with statistical and factual information.
gazetteer: A geographical dictionary or index of place names
From gazette. Reference book listing geographical places with descriptions, used in Indian administration.
posthumous: A book published after the death of its author
From Latin postumus (last). Work released after the author's death, like some of Premchand's writings.
pseudonymous: A work published under a false name
Pseudo (false) + onyma (name). Written under a pen name, like George Orwell (real name Eric Blair).
apocryphal: A story or statement of doubtful authenticity
From Greek apokryphos (hidden). Of questionable origin or truthfulness, widely circulated but unverified.
manuscript: A handwritten document, especially an author's original text
Manu (hand) + scriptus (written). Original handwritten or typed text before publication.
facsimile: An exact copy or reproduction of a document or artwork
From Latin fac simile (make similar). Exact reproduction, also the technology behind fax machines.
appendix: A supplement added at the end of a book or document
From Latin appendere (to hang upon). Additional material at the end providing supplementary information.
addendum: Something added to a document after it has been completed
From Latin addere (to add). Addition or supplement to a finished work, plural: addenda.
corrigendum: A correction of errors in a printed book or document
From Latin corrigere (to correct). List of corrections for errors found after publication, plural: corrigenda.
isthmus: A narrow strip of land connecting two larger land masses
From Greek isthmos (neck). Land bridge between two larger areas, like the Isthmus of Kra.
peninsula: A piece of land almost surrounded by water on three sides
Paene (almost) + insula (island). Land mass projecting into water, like the Indian Peninsula.
strait: A narrow passage of water connecting two large bodies of water
From Latin strictus (narrow). Waterway between two land masses, like the Palk Strait near India.
atoll: A ring-shaped coral reef enclosing a lagoon
From Maldivian atolu. Circular coral island with a central lagoon, common in Lakshadweep.
oasis: A fertile area in a desert where water is found
From Egyptian wahe (dwelling place). Green spot in a desert sustained by underground water.
delta: A triangular area of land at the mouth of a river
Named after Greek letter delta (triangle). Sediment deposit where river meets sea, like the Sundarbans Delta.
estuary: The wide mouth of a river where it meets the sea
From Latin aestuarium (tidal inlet). Where freshwater mixes with saltwater, like the Narmada Estuary.
tributary: A stream or river flowing into a larger river
From Latin tributum (contribution). River feeding into a main river, like Yamuna is a tributary of Ganga.
vegan: A person who eats no animal products at all
Coined from 'vegetarian'. One who avoids all animal-derived food including dairy, eggs, and honey.
fruitarian: A person who eats only fruits, nuts, and seeds
From fruit + arian. One who consumes only plant products harvested without killing the plant.
gourmet: A lover of good food and drink
From French gourmet (wine broker). A connoisseur who appreciates fine food and culinary excellence.
gourmand: A person who eats and drinks excessively for pleasure
From French gourmand (greedy eater). One who enjoys eating large quantities, less refined than a gourmet.
portrait: A painting of a person, especially the face
From French portraire (to depict). Artistic representation of a person, capturing their likeness.
mural: A painting or picture on a wall or ceiling
From Latin murus (wall). Large artwork painted directly on a wall surface, like Ajanta cave paintings.
mosaic: A picture made by arranging small pieces of coloured stone or glass
From Greek mouseion (of the Muses). Art form using tiny pieces to create patterns and images.
fresco: A painting done on wet plaster on a wall
From Italian fresco (fresh). Painting technique on freshly laid plaster, used in ancient Indian caves.
sculpture: The art of making figures by carving stone or moulding clay
From Latin sculpere (to carve). Three-dimensional art, like the sculptures of Khajuraho or Mahabalipuram.
pottery: The art of making pots and dishes from clay by baking
From Old English pott (container). Craft of creating vessels from clay, a traditional Indian craft.
exile: A state of being away from one's own country
From Latin exsilium (banishment). Forced or voluntary absence from homeland, like the Dalai Lama in India.
asylum: Protection or shelter given by a country to someone fleeing danger
From Greek asylon (sanctuary). Legal protection granted to those fleeing persecution from their home country.
quarantine: A period of being kept away from others to prevent disease from spreading
From Italian quarantina (forty days). Enforced isolation to stop contagious disease spreading.
curfew: A restriction on people's movement, typically at night
From French couvre-feu (cover fire). Order requiring people to stay indoors during specified hours.
voice: The sound made by the vibration of vocal cords during speech
From Latin vox (voice). Sound produced by the larynx for speaking and singing.
echo: The repetition of a sound caused by reflection of sound waves
From Greek Ekho (nymph who could only repeat). Sound bouncing back from a surface.
umbra: The complete shadow cast during an eclipse
From Latin umbra (shadow). The darkest central part of a shadow where light is completely blocked.
penumbra: The partial shadow around the complete shadow during an eclipse
Paene (almost) + umbra (shadow). The lighter outer region of a shadow where light is partially blocked.
catalyst: A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed
From Greek katalysis (dissolution). Agent that accelerates change without itself being altered.
solvent: A substance that dissolves another substance to form a solution
From Latin solvere (to loosen). Liquid in which another substance dissolves, like water is a universal solvent.
corrosive: A substance that can burn or destroy organic tissue by chemical action
From Latin corrodere (to gnaw). Chemical that damages or eats away material on contact, like acid.
reagent: A substance that produces a chemical reaction when added to another substance
From Latin reagere (to react). Chemical used to cause a reaction, common in laboratory analysis.
pandemic: A disease that spreads over a whole country or the entire world
Pan (all) + demos (people). Disease affecting a vast population across countries, like COVID-19.
epidemic: A disease that spreads rapidly among many people in a particular area
Epi (upon) + demos (people). Rapid spread of disease in a specific region or community.
endemic: A disease regularly found in a particular area or among a particular group
En (in) + demos (people). Disease constantly present in a region, like malaria in parts of India.
antidote: A medicine that works against the effects of a poison
Anti (against) + doton (given). Substance that counteracts the harmful effects of a poison or toxin.
antibiotic: A medicine that kills or stops the growth of bacteria
Anti (against) + bios (life). Drug that fights bacterial infections, like penicillin.
antiseptic: A substance applied to the skin to prevent infection
Anti (against) + septikos (putrefying). Agent preventing growth of germs on living tissue.
analgesic: A medicine that relieves pain without causing loss of consciousness
An (without) + algos (pain). Painkiller like paracetamol that reduces pain without inducing unconsciousness.
anesthetic: A substance that causes loss of sensation or consciousness
An (without) + aisthesis (sensation). Drug used in surgery to block pain, either local or general.
sedative: A medicine that calms and induces sleep
From Latin sedare (to calm). Drug that reduces anxiety and promotes drowsiness or sleep.
stimulant: A substance that stimulates the body and increases activity
From Latin stimulare (to goad). Agent that increases alertness and energy, like caffeine.
placebo: An inactive substance given as medicine to satisfy a patient psychologically
From Latin placebo (I shall please). Fake treatment used in clinical trials to test real drugs.
panacea: A supposed cure for all diseases or problems
Pan (all) + akos (remedy). A universal solution — often used to criticise unrealistic promises.
pharmacopoeia: The study and practice of preparing and dispensing medicines
Pharmakon (drug) + poiein (to make). Official publication listing approved medicines and their standards.
injunction: A court order restraining a person from a specific act
From Latin injungere (to enjoin). Judicial order prohibiting or compelling an action, common in PIL cases.
subpoena: A written order requiring a person to appear in court
From Latin sub poena (under penalty). Legal order compelling testimony or production of evidence.
warrant: A legal document authorising police to make an arrest or search
From Old French warant (protector). Written authorisation issued by a judge for arrest or search.
acquittal: A judgment by a court that a person is not guilty
From Latin acquietare (to set at rest). Court's finding of innocence, freeing the accused of charges.
perjury: A false statement made under oath in court
From Latin perjurium (false oath). Crime of lying while sworn to tell the truth before a court.
libel: A written statement that falsely damages a person's reputation
From Latin libellus (small book). Published false statement harming someone's reputation — written defamation.
slander: A spoken statement that falsely damages a person's reputation
From Old French esclandre (scandal). Verbal false statement harming reputation — spoken defamation.
forgery: The crime of making a false document or signature
From Latin fabricare (to fabricate). Creating fake documents, currency, or signatures with intent to deceive.
plagiarism: Using someone else's work or ideas and presenting them as your own
From Latin plagiarius (kidnapper). Intellectual theft — passing off another's writing or ideas as original.
temple: A place of worship for Hindus
From Latin templum (sacred space). Structure dedicated to Hindu deities for prayer and rituals.
mosque: A place of worship for Muslims
From Arabic masjid (place of prostration). Building where Muslims gather for congregational prayers.
synagogue: A place of worship for Jews
From Greek synagoge (assembly). Jewish house of worship and community gathering.
gurudwara: A place of worship for Sikhs
Guru (teacher) + dwara (door/gateway). Sikh house of worship, meaning 'gateway to the Guru'.
stupa: A Buddhist shrine, typically a dome-shaped structure
From Sanskrit stupa (heap). Mound-like structure containing Buddhist relics, like Sanchi Stupa.
pagoda: A Buddhist monastery or temple, especially in Southeast and East Asia
From Portuguese pagode. Tiered tower associated with Buddhism, common in Myanmar, China, and Japan.
vihara: A Buddhist residential monastery for monks
From Sanskrit vihara (dwelling). Ancient Buddhist monastery, like the Nalanda Mahavihara.
pantheism: Belief that God exists in all parts of nature and the universe
Pan (all) + theos (god). Doctrine that God and nature are identical — the universe itself is divine.
monotheism: Mono (one) + theos (god). Religions like Islam and Sikhism that worship a single supreme deity.
polytheism: Poly (many) + theos (god). Worship of multiple deities, as in aspects of Hinduism and ancient Greek religion.
monandry: The practice of a man having only one wife
Mono (one) + aner/andros (man/husband). A woman having only one husband at a time.
hierocracy: A system of government in which priests rule in the name of God
Hieros (sacred) + kratos (rule). Priestly government — closely related to but distinct from theocracy.
agnosticism: The belief that nothing can be known about the nature of God
A (not) + gnostos (known). Position that the existence or nature of God is unknowable.
pacifism: The belief that war and violence are unjustifiable
From Latin pax (peace). Opposition to war and violence as means of settling disputes, as practised by Gandhi.
nihilism: The belief that nothing in life has meaning or value
From Latin nihil (nothing). Philosophical view rejecting all moral and religious principles.
empiricism: The belief that knowledge comes only from practical experience
From Greek empeiria (experience). Philosophical doctrine that experience is the source of all knowledge.
rationalism: The belief that reason is the chief source of knowledge
From Latin ratio (reason). Philosophical view that reason and logic are the primary sources of knowledge.
patrilineal: A system of social organisation in which the father is head of the family
Pater (father) + linea (line). Descent and inheritance traced through the male line.
matrilineal: A system of social organisation in which descent is traced through the mother
Mater (mother) + linea (line). Lineage through the female line, as in Khasi and Nair communities of India.
endogamy: Marriage within one's own community, caste, or social group
Endo (within) + gamos (marriage). Practice of marrying within a specific social group, common in Indian caste system.
exogamy: Marriage outside one's own community, caste, or social group
Exo (outside) + gamos (marriage). Practice of marrying outside one's social group or clan.
iconoclasm: The deliberate and systematic destruction of cultural or religious heritage
Eikon (image) + klan (to break). Destruction of religious images or established beliefs and institutions.
sacrilege: An act of treating a sacred place or object with violent disrespect
Sacer (sacred) + legere (to steal). Violation or misuse of something considered holy or sacred.
blasphemy: Speech or action showing contempt for God or sacred things
From Greek blasphemia (evil-speaking). Irreverent speech about God or religious matters.
amphibian: An animal that lives both on land and in water
Amphi (both) + bios (life). Creatures like frogs that live in water and on land.
nocturnal: An animal that is active during the night
From Latin nox (night). Animals like owls and bats that are active after dark.
diurnal: An animal that is active during the day
From Latin dies (day). Animals like eagles and butterflies that are active in daylight.
arboreal: An animal that lives on trees
From Latin arbor (tree). Tree-dwelling creatures like monkeys and squirrels.
aquatic: An animal that lives in water
From Latin aqua (water). Organisms living entirely in water, like fish and dolphins.
terrestrial: An animal that lives on land
From Latin terra (earth). Organisms living primarily on land, like tigers and elephants.
scavenger: An animal that feeds on dead and decaying flesh
From Old French escauver (to inspect). Creature feeding on carrion, like vultures and hyenas.
predator: An animal that hunts and kills other animals for food
From Latin praedator (plunderer). Hunter at the top of the food chain, like tigers and eagles.
parasite: An organism that lives on or inside another organism and benefits at its expense
From Greek parasitos (one who eats at another's table). Organism surviving at the host's cost.
symbiosis: A relationship where two different organisms live together and both benefit
Syn (together) + bios (life). Mutually beneficial partnership, like lichen (fungus + algae).
migration: The seasonal movement of animals from one region to another
From Latin migrare (to move). Periodic travel of birds or animals, like Siberian cranes visiting India.
hibernation: The state of deep sleep in which some animals pass the winter
From Latin hibernare (to winter). Prolonged torpor during cold months, like bears in winter.
estivation: The state of deep sleep in which some animals pass the summer
From Latin aestivare (to spend the summer). Summer dormancy to survive extreme heat and drought.
moulting: The shedding of skin, feathers, or fur by an animal
From Latin mutare (to change). Natural process of replacing outer covering, as snakes shed skin.
metamorphosis: The process of transformation from an immature form to an adult form
Meta (change) + morphe (form). Dramatic physical change, like caterpillar becoming a butterfly.
insomniac: A person who is unable to sleep
In (not) + somnus (sleep). One who suffers from chronic inability to fall or stay asleep.
narcoleptic: A person who has an irresistible tendency to fall asleep suddenly
Narke (numbness) + lepsis (seizure). One suffering from uncontrollable episodes of sudden sleep.
apathetic: A person who is indifferent to both pleasure and pain
A (without) + pathos (feeling). One showing no interest, enthusiasm, or concern.
pessimism: A tendency to see the worst aspect of things or believe the worst will happen
From Latin pessimus (worst). Disposition to expect unfavourable outcomes in all situations.
optimism: A tendency to see the best in things and expect favourable outcomes
From Latin optimus (best). Disposition to expect the most favourable result.
guerrilla: Warfare carried out by small independent groups using surprise attacks
From Spanish guerrilla (little war). Irregular combat using hit-and-run tactics by small mobile forces.
siege: A prolonged military blockade of a city or fortress to force surrender
From Latin sedere (to sit). Surrounding and cutting off supplies to compel capitulation.
ambush: A surprise attack from a concealed position
From Old French embuscher (to hide in bushes). Sudden attack by troops lying in wait.
sabotage: Deliberate destruction of equipment or property to hinder an enemy
From French saboter (to clatter with wooden shoes). Intentional disruption of operations or infrastructure.
rampart: A strong defensive wall surrounding a castle or city
From French rempart (fortification). Protective wall built around forts, like those of Rajasthan's forts.
moat: A deep wide ditch filled with water surrounding a castle or fort
From Old French mote (mound). Water-filled trench for defense, seen in many Indian forts.
turret: A small tower at the corner of a building or wall
From Old French tourete (small tower). Projecting tower on a castle wall for observation and defense.
spire: A tall pointed structure on top of a tower or building
From Old English spir (sprout). Tapering cone or pyramid atop a church tower or monument.
dome: A rounded vault forming the roof of a building
From Latin domus (house). Hemispherical roof structure, like the dome of the Taj Mahal.
arch: A curved structure supporting the weight above an opening
From Latin arcus (bow). Curved construction spanning a doorway or window, like the Gateway of India.
pillar: A tall vertical structure used as a support in a building
From Latin pila (stone barrier). Upright support structure, like the Ashoka Pillars across India.
fictitious: Something that exists or happens only in the imagination
From Latin fictus (formed/feigned). Invented or made up, not real or true.
authentic: Something that is genuine and not copied or forged
From Greek authentikos (original). Verified as real and trustworthy, not a fake.
spurious: Something that is not genuine though appearing to be so
From Latin spurius (illegitimate). False or fake while seeming real, like spurious medicines.
counterfeit: An exact copy made to deceive, especially of money or goods
From Latin contrafacere (to imitate). Fraudulent imitation, especially of currency or branded goods.
souvenir: A thing that is kept as a reminder of a person, place, or event
From French souvenir (to remember). Keepsake purchased or collected during travel or events.
relic: An object surviving from the past, often of historical interest
From Latin reliquiae (remains). Something surviving from an earlier time, like archaeological relics.
heirloom: A valuable object passed down through generations in a family
Heir + loom (tool/possession). Treasured family possession inherited across generations.
deterrent: A person or thing serving as a warning to others
From Latin deterrere (to frighten away). Something that discourages action through fear of consequences.
incentive: Something that motivates or encourages a person to act
From Latin incentivum (setting the tune). Reward or motivation encouraging desired behaviour or action.
impediment: Something that blocks or hinders progress
From Latin impedire (to shackle the feet). Obstacle that delays or prevents progress.
pandemonium: A state of confusion and noisy disturbance
Pan (all) + daimon (demon). Wild uproar and chaos, coined by Milton for the capital of Hell in Paradise Lost.
tranquility: A state of perfect peace and calm
From Latin tranquillus (calm). State of peaceful quietness and serenity.
homonym: Words that are exactly the same in spelling and pronunciation but different in meaning
Homo (same) + onyma (name). Words like 'bank' (river bank vs financial bank) with identical form but different meaning.
homophone: Words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings
Homo (same) + phone (sound). Words like 'there/their/they're' that sound alike but differ in meaning and spelling.
antonym: Words that are opposite in meaning
Anti (opposite) + onyma (name). Words with contrary meanings, like 'hot' and 'cold'.
autodidact: A person who teaches himself without formal instruction
Auto (self) + didaktos (taught). A self-taught person who learns independently without a teacher.
pedagogue: A teacher, especially a strict or pedantic one
Paidos (child) + agogos (leader). Originally a slave who led children to school; now a formal teacher.
pedagogy: The art or science of teaching
Paidos (child) + agoge (leading). Methods and practices of instruction and education.
andragogy: The method and practice of teaching adult learners
Aner/andros (man) + agoge (leading). Adult education theory, distinct from child-focused pedagogy.
prodigy: A child with exceptional talent or ability
From Latin prodigium (omen/portent). A young person with extraordinary abilities, especially in music or maths.
savant: A person with exceptional knowledge in a specific area, sometimes despite other limitations
From French savoir (to know). One with profound expertise in a narrow field.
sign language: A system of communication using gestures and signs instead of speech
Visual language using hand shapes, movements, and facial expressions for the hearing-impaired.
braille: A system of reading and writing for the blind using raised dots
Named after Louis Braille. Tactile writing system of raised dots read by touch.
semaphore: A system of signalling using flags or lights held in different positions
From Greek sema (sign) + phoros (bearing). Visual signalling system used in naval communication.
phenomenon: A thing that can be seen or known but not explained
From Greek phainomenon (thing appearing). An observable fact or event, especially one not fully understood.
anomaly: Something that deviates from what is normal or expected
From Greek anomalia (unevenness). An irregularity or deviation from the common rule.
enigma: A person or thing that is mysterious and difficult to understand
From Greek ainigma (riddle). Something puzzling and difficult to interpret or understand.
famine: The extreme scarcity of food in a region leading to starvation
From Latin fames (hunger). Severe food shortage causing widespread hunger and death, like the Bengal Famine of 1943.
drought: A prolonged period of abnormally low rainfall
From Old English drugoth (dryness). Extended dry period causing water scarcity, common in parts of Maharashtra and Rajasthan.
deluge: A great flood or heavy downpour of rain
From Latin diluvium (flood). Overwhelming flood or torrential rainfall causing destruction.
cyclone: A violent storm with very strong circular winds
From Greek kyklon (moving in a circle). Rotating windstorm, like cyclones that hit India's eastern coast.
earthquake: A shaking of the earth's surface caused by underground movement
Earth + quake (to shake). Sudden release of energy in the earth's crust, measured on the Richter scale.
avalanche: A large mass of snow and ice falling rapidly down a mountainside
From French avalanche. Sudden slide of snow and rock down a slope, common in the Himalayas.
glacier: A slow-moving mass of ice formed by accumulated snow in mountains
From French glace (ice). Massive ice body moving slowly downhill, like the Siachen Glacier.
iceberg: A large floating mass of ice detached from a glacier
From Dutch ijsberg (ice mountain). Huge ice chunk floating in the sea, mostly submerged.
tornado: From Spanish tornar (to turn). Rapidly rotating column of air extending from a thunderstorm to the ground.
athazagoraphobia: An abnormal fear of being forgotten or ignored
From Greek athazagora (forget). Anxiety about being forgotten by loved ones or society.
fluency: The ability to speak effortlessly and smoothly
From Latin fluere (to flow). Ability to express oneself easily and articulately in a language.
verbosity: The use of more words than necessary to express an idea
From Latin verbosus (wordy). Excessive use of words, opposite of brevity and conciseness.
brevity: Concise and exact use of words in speech or writing
From Latin brevis (short). Quality of expressing ideas in few words — 'brevity is the soul of wit'.
neologism: A word newly coined or a new meaning given to an existing word
Neo (new) + logos (word). Newly invented word like 'selfie' or 'blog' entering common usage.
archaism: The use of old or outdated words and expressions
From Greek arkhaios (ancient). Use of words that are no longer in everyday speech, like 'thou' or 'hath'.
jargon: Special words or expressions used by a particular profession or group
From Old French jargon (twittering). Technical language specific to a profession, like legal or medical jargon.
slang: Very informal language used in casual conversation but not in formal writing
Origin uncertain. Informal words and phrases used in everyday speech, constantly evolving.
colloquial: Language used in ordinary conversation but not in formal speech or writing
From Latin colloquium (conversation). Informal everyday language, more accepted than slang.
vernacular: The native language or dialect of a specific region
From Latin vernaculus (domestic). Language spoken by ordinary people of a region, like Hindi or Marathi.
dialect: A regional variety of a language differing in pronunciation, grammar, or vocabulary
From Greek dialektos (discourse). Local form of a language, like Bhojpuri or Marwari.
odyssey: From Homer's epic about Odysseus. A prolonged adventurous journey full of challenges.
expedition: A journey to a distant or unexplored region for a specific purpose
From Latin expedire (to make ready). Organised journey for exploration, research, or military purpose.
voyage: From French voyage (journey). Travel across water, like Vasco da Gama's voyage to India.
antibody: A substance in the blood that fights disease
Anti (against) + body. Protein produced by the immune system to neutralise pathogens.
antigen: A foreign substance that triggers an immune response in the body
Anti(body) + gen (producing). Molecule that stimulates production of antibodies, like a virus protein.
antioxidant: A substance that slows down or prevents the oxidation of other substances
Anti (against) + oxidant. Compound protecting cells from damage, found in fruits and vegetables.
spectator: A person who is present at an event but does not take part
From Latin spectare (to watch). One who watches an event, especially a sport or public occasion.
witness: A person who sees an event happen, especially a crime or accident
From Old English witnes (knowledge). One who observes an event and can give testimony about it.
bystander: A person who happens to be near an event but is not directly involved
By + stander. One present at an incident without participating, often used in 'bystander effect'.
affidavit: A written declaration made under oath for use as evidence in court
From Latin affidare (to pledge faith). Sworn written statement used as legal evidence in Indian courts.
ordinance: A government order temporarily having the force of law, issued when Parliament is not in session
From Latin ordinare (to arrange). In India, issued by the President under Article 123 of the Constitution.
amendment: A formal change or addition to a law or constitution
From Latin emendare (to correct). Modification to the Constitution, like the 42nd or 44th Amendment of India.
statute: An established law or rule made by a legislative body
From Latin statutum (thing set up). Written law passed by Parliament, as distinct from common law.
copyright: The exclusive legal right of an author to reproduce and sell their creative work
Copy + right. Legal protection for literary, musical, and artistic works under the Copyright Act of India.
patent: An exclusive right granted by the government to an inventor for a new invention
From Latin patere (to lay open). Legal protection for inventions, granted by the Indian Patent Office.
